title: Spike in p95/p99 on Hosted API — request queue saturation
preview:
From: Samantha Nguyen <samantha@aurahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 09:15:42 -0800
Subject: Sudden p95/p99 jump this morning — urgent

Hi Ben,

We're seeing a significant degradation in response times from Redwood's Hosted API starting ~08:45 PT. Pre-incident our p95 sat around 320-450ms for our gen calls; since the spike p95 is hovering ~2.0–2.6s and p99 is spiking above 4s. Throughput is still high on our side, but a lot of user-facing requests are timing out (we're observing many client retries).

I attached a sample of our request IDs and timestamps (aura_latency_sample_0108-0110.csv). Notably, the error rate jumped from 0.2% -> 1.8% with 503s and 504 gateway errors interspersed. We haven't changed our model selection or prompt patterns. Can you confirm if anything changed on your side (batching defaults, deployment rollouts, routing policy)?

This is causing material user impact for our production cohort. We need an immediate mitigation and an ETA for a permanent fix.

Thanks,
Samantha Nguyen
Head of Platform
AuraHealth

--
Attachments: aura_latency_sample_0108-0110.csv (csv)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Nguyen <samantha@aurahealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 09:26:10 -0800
Subject: Re: Sudden p95/p99 jump this morning — urgent

Samantha —

Thanks for the data and the heads up. I'm escalating this immediately to our on-call and engineering leads (cc'd). We see your traffic in the dashboard and I've opened PD-incident: INC-20280112-742.

Immediate asks from our side:
- Can you confirm if you increased concurrency from the client side or added any parallel fan-out in the last 24 hours?
- Are those request_ids in the CSV the ones showing timeouts on your client?

We're activating an emergency mitigation lane and will update you in the next 30 minutes with steps we can take (short-term) and a target for a deeper root-cause analysis.

I also propose a bridge at 10:00 PT — let me know if that works.

—Ben
Director, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

--
Quoted message follows:
> From: Samantha Nguyen <samantha@aurahealth.com>
> Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 09:15:42 -0800
> Subject: Sudden p95/p99 jump this morning — urgent
>
> (see above)

From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.ai>
Cc: 
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 09:39:05 -0800
Subject: RE: Sudden p95/p99 jump this morning — urgent

Ben / Vivek —

I've started a quick triage against the metrics linked below and pulled the logs referenced in the PD incident. Initial findings:

- Host-level metrics: overall capacity is nominal for the cluster handling your region; GPU utilization is within expected ranges.
- Queue metrics: there's a sustained rise in per-customer queue depth for AuraHealth starting 08:42 PT (queue depth 6x baseline).
- Batching behavior: we see an increase in batch sizes for the model variant you use (redwood-gpt-large-v1) — median batch tokens rose ~2.8x between 08:30–09:00.
- KV/prefix cache: higher miss rate for recent requests (cache miss rate up ~18%), causing more cold-run compute.
- Request patterns: multiple in-flight parallel requests from a handful of your API keys — likely client-side fan-out.

Immediate risk vector: the combination of larger-than-normal batches and deeper per-customer queues is increasing service time for queued requests; the scheduler's latency SLO smoothing is letting larger batches form to optimize throughput which unfortunately increases p95 for latency-sensitive requests.

Short-term mitigations I propose we can apply in the next 10–20 minutes (low risk):
1) Enforce a per-customer max_concurrency override to limit in-flight requests to 6 for AuraHealth (reduces queue piling).
2) Temporarily cap max_batch_tokens for the customer's routing policy to 512 tokens (current is 2048).
3) Enable immediate fallback routing to a lower-latency model variant (redwood-gpt-medium-v1) for requests flagged as latency-sensitive.
4) Purge prefix cache entries for the busiest customer keys to reduce KV cold-run churn, but do this only if the cache TTL is short — I'll coordinate.

I'll apply #1 and #2 proactively now and monitor. If those stabilize p95 we'll keep them and build a safer tuning. If not, we'll route affected keys to a warm dedicated pool.

I'll post a running update in PD-incident and attach extracted logs.

Monica
SRE, Redwood Inference

--
Attachments referenced: hostedapi_debug_01012_logs.tar.gz
Related dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/dashboards/hostedapi/p95?customer=AuraHealth
PD Incident: INC-20280112-742

From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 09:51:40 -0800
Subject: RE: Sudden p95/p99 jump this morning — urgent

Thanks Monica — quick operational notes:

- Go ahead with the per-customer concurrency cap and batch token cap. I also recommend a temporary routing weight change to send 25% of AuraHealth traffic to the Dedicated warm pool (if they have reserved capacity) — this will shed load from the shared pool and reduce queuing while we investigate.
- If the dedicated pool isn't available, drop-in a soft-fail policy that returns a 429 with Retry-After for non-interactive endpoints so clients back off rather than piling retries.
- Post-mitigation, we should run a short controlled A/B to see the latency vs cost trade-off before restoring defaults.

I'll stand by to make routing changes and can flip the dedicated routing within 8 minutes of approval.

-Vivek
Engineering Lead, Serving Runtime

--
Quoted: Monica's note

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Nguyen <samantha@aurahealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 10:05:18 -0800
Subject: Re: Sudden p95/p99 jump this morning — urgent

Samantha —

Thanks for the patience. Engineering has applied two mitigations: per-key concurrency capped at 6 and max_batch_tokens reduced to 512 for your routing. We also triaged to reduce batching formation for latency-sensitive routes. Initial observations: p95 trending down from ~2.2s to ~750ms in the first 12 minutes. We're watching for any error-rate regressions.

Operationally, Vivek can shift a fraction of your traffic to a warm dedicated pool if you want immediate further isolation — this has a small cost implication but will keep p95 closer to pre-incident levels while we complete RCA. If you'd prefer, we can keep the caps in place and run a deeper tuning pass over the next 48 hours.

As next steps:
- Short bridge at 10:30 PT to review options (I will send calendar invite).
- We will provide a post-incident summary with root cause and recommended permanent config changes.
- As a gesture of goodwill for the customer impact, I'm opening a request for a service credit for the affected window for your account (DH-9821-AURA).

Can you confirm whether you'd like traffic split to dedicated pool now or prefer caps-only?

—Ben

--
Attachments: mitigation_plan_snippet.txt
Related: https://hubspot.com/deals/9821

> On Tue, Jan 10, 2028 at 09:39, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai> wrote:
> (triage summary)

From: Samantha Nguyen <samantha@aurahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: support+aurasvc@example.com
Date: Wed, 12 Jan 2028 18:02:10 -0800
Subject: RE: Sudden p95/p99 jump this morning — urgent

Ben / team —

Thanks for the quick response and the mitigation. We've observed the p95 drop into the 650–800ms band after your changes and the error rate has normalized. The shift to dedicated routing isn't necessary immediately for us provided the caps stay in place while you do root-cause analysis.

We'd like a short postmortem once available and confirmation of the service credit you mentioned. Also, please include any recommendations on client-side best practices to avoid queue saturation (we suspect a fan-out pattern in a new background job triggered last week).

Appreciate the rapid turnaround.

—Samantha
Head of Platform
AuraHealth

--
Quoted: Ben's note re: options

title: Dedicated capacity + burst/autoscale negotiation — Helios AI
preview:
From: Emma Shaw <emma@helios.ai>
To: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>
Cc: capacity-team@helios.ai
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Dedicated capacity inquiry — initial requirements (Helios)

Hi Stephanie,

Thanks for the product overview earlier. We're moving ahead internally and would like to nail down Dedicated sizing and the burst/autoscale guardrails for an upcoming pilot. High-level asks below — attaching a short requirements doc (helios_req_v1.pdf).

Summary of our workload:
- Mixed chat + completion workloads (50% chat flows, 50% batch generation)
- Baseline: 12 req/s sustained; Peak: 180 req/s (5–10 minute spikes)
- Typical sequence length: 512 tokens; bursts often include longer context up to 2k tokens
- Model family: llama-3-16b / quantized variant (preference for 8-bit/4-bit)

Questions we want to resolve:
1) How quickly can Redwood support a 15x short-lived burst above baseline without violating SLAs?
2) How are billing and credits handled for transient capacity?
3) Warmup/KV cache behavior during rapid scale-ups — what performance hit should we expect?

We'd like a technical sync this week to validate assumptions and get a rough SOW. Can you loop in whoever owns the burst/autoscale policies?

Thanks,
Emma Shaw
Head of ML Systems — Helios AI

Attachment: helios_req_v1.pdf


From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Shaw <emma@helios.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 10 Jan 2028 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity inquiry — initial requirements (Helios)

Emma — thanks for the clear summary and the attachment. Ben and Priyom are cc'd — Ben will lead the technical sizing, Priyom will handle commercial/contract items.

Quick answers up front:
- Short-lived bursts: we can provision an ephemeral shoulder pool to cover ~12–20x baseline for 10 minute windows with a mix of dedicated and ephemeral GPUs. Effective latency SLOs depend on cache warmup; expect a 1.2–1.5x token latency premium during cold bursts.
- Billing: we support a burst-credit model (preloaded credits + on-demand overage) or a soft-burst SLA with capped overage rates. Priyom can share term options.
- KV cache/warmup: Significant for longer contexts. We recommend a small warm standby of pre-warmed instances to reduce the worst-case penalty.

Proposed next steps:
1) Technical sync — 60 minutes: Thurs 13 Jan 10:00 PT?
2) Ben to propose an initial capacity profile & autoscale policy (will include headroom multipliers, pre-warm thresholds, and eviction order).
3) Priyom to share preliminary commercial options and a term-sheet.

Ben — can you draft the capacity profile and attach a term-sheet we can iterate on?

Best,
Stephanie Nguyen
Senior Account Executive — Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Shaw <emma@helios.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 11 Jan 2028 08:45:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity inquiry — initial requirements (Helios)

Emma / Stephanie — attaching a draft term sheet (redwood-dedicated-burst-term-sheet.xlsx) and initial sizing notes. High-level proposal below.

Proposal (initial, for discussion):
- Baseline Dedicated Pool: 16x A100-like GPUs (pinned) sized to cover 12 req/s sustained at p95 latency targets for typical jobs.
- Shoulder/Ephemeral Pool: up to 240 vGPU-equivalents that can be spun up as ephemeral instances across spot/ephemeral-capacity-backed nodes. This gives ~15x spike headroom.
- Pre-warm band: maintain 8 warm instances (model-dedicated) to reduce cold-start penalty for KV cache — these are warmed by a rolling keep-alive traffic pattern.
- Autoscale policy (sketch):
  * Scale-up trigger: moving average of queue length + 95th token-p99 latency crossing threshold for 2 consecutive 30s windows.
  * Fast path: allocate ephemeral in 30–60s (provisioning dependent on region); for very short spikes (< 2 min) route to shoulder pool prioritizing lower-memory models to keep latency acceptable.
  * Slow path: if spike persists > 10m, promote ephemeral to persistent until safe to decommission.
  * Eviction priority: opportunistic workloads -> batch generation -> chat sessions (chat has sticky KV cache)
- Throttle strategy: graceful shedding with a 10% error backoff and queueing fallback to a lower-cost model variant when headroom exhausted.

Operational metrics to publish in the SOW:
- Provisioning latency (median/90/99) for ephemeral nodes
- Cache sync time for 512/2k contexts
- Token-level cost delta for bursts vs baseline

Caveats: longer contexts up to 2k will increase memory pressure and slower warmup — we should build a small staging test for a 2k warmup profile.

Attachment: redwood-dedicated-burst-term-sheet.xlsx

Happy to walk through this on the sync.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Director, Infrastructure Product — Redwood Inference


From: Emma Shaw <emma@helios.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 11 Jan 2028 10:05:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity inquiry — initial requirements (Helios)

Thanks Ben — this is very helpful. A few clarifying items before the sync:

1) Provisioning latency: you list 30–60s for ephemeral allocation — is that cold-start only, or does it include model load time for a 16B quantized checkpoint with 2k context?
2) Pre-warm sizing: what CPU/memory overhead do you see for maintaining 8 warm instances (we need to justify reserved spend internally)?
3) Throttling/fallback: when you say "lower-cost model variant", do you mean an internally hosted smaller model or a Redwood-managed quantized fallback? How do you guarantee output quality / deterministic fallbacks?

Helios peak distribution: most spikes are bursty for ~6–8 minutes and account for ~18% of daily token volume. If we can cover 12–18 minute windows reliably, that would be fine.

One more ask: procurement needs the billing treatment spelled out (how credits are drawn down, overage unit rates, and an example monthly invoice). Can your team include a sample invoice?

—Emma

From: Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Shaw <emma@helios.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 12 Jan 2028 09:20:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity inquiry — initial requirements (Helios)

Emma — looping in procurement questions and a commercial summary. Attached is an initial SOW draft (sow-draft-v0.3.docx). Key commercial points: 

- Billing model options:
  1) Prepaid burst credits: Helios prepays for a block of burst credits that consume at a higher per-token rate during a spike; credits are deducted first, then on-demand overage is billed monthly.
  2) Soft-burst SLA: Redwood guarantees X minutes of burst coverage per month at a committed monthly fee, with capped overage beyond that.

- Sample invoice: included in SOW — shows baseline committed capacity, credit consumption, and an overage example for a 12-minute 15x spike.
- Contract language: we recommend a clause for graceful degradation and a joint test plan for verifying warmup metrics during onboarding.

Next actions:
- We can finalize a commercial path after the technical sync Thurs 13 Jan 10:00 PT.
- Post-sync: Ben will provide a short runbook for on-call/ops for burst events.

Look forward to the meeting.

Regards,
Priyom Das
Lead, Sales Operations — Redwood Inference

Attachment: sow-draft-v0.3.docx

Cc: Priyom Das <priyom@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 11 Jan 2028 08:45:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity inquiry — initial requirements (Helios)

Emma / Stephanie — attaching a draft term sheet (redwood-dedicated-burst-term-sheet.xlsx) and initial sizing notes. High-level proposal below.

Proposal (initial, for discussion):
- Baseline Dedicated Pool: 16x A100-like GPUs (pinned) sized to cover 12 req/s sustained at p95 latency targets for typical jobs.
- Shoulder/Ephemeral Pool: up to 240 vGPU-equivalents that can be spun up as ephemeral instances across spot/ephemeral-capacity-backed nodes. This gives ~15x spike headroom.
- Pre-warm band: maintain 8 warm instances (model-dedicated) to reduce cold-start penalty for KV cache — these are warmed by a rolling keep-alive traffic pattern.
- Autoscale policy (sketch):
  * Scale-up trigger: moving average of queue length + 95th token-p99 latency crossing threshold for 2 consecutive 30s windows.
  * Fast path: allocate ephemeral in 30–60s (provisioning dependent on region); for very short spikes (< 2 min) route to shoulder pool prioritizing lower-memory models to keep latency acceptable.
  * Slow path: if spike persists > 10m, promote ephemeral to persistent until safe to decommission.
  * Eviction priority: opportunistic workloads -> batch generation -> chat sessions (chat has sticky KV cache)
- Throttle strategy: graceful shedding with a 10% error backoff and queueing fallback to a lower-cost model variant when headroom exhausted.

Operational metrics to publish in the SOW:
- Provisioning latency (median/90/99) for ephemeral nodes
- Cache sync time for 512/2k contexts
- Token-level cost delta for bursts vs baseline

Caveats: longer contexts up to 2k will increase memory pressure and slower warmup — we should build a small staging test for a 2k warmup profile.

Attachment: redwood-dedicated-burst-term-sheet.xlsx

Happy to walk through this on the sync.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Director, Infrastructure Product — Redwood Inference

1) Provisioning latency: you list 30–60s for ephemeral allocation — is that cold-start only, or does it include model load time for a 16B quantized checkpoint with 2k context?
2) Pre-warm sizing: what CPU/memory overhead do you see for maintaining 8 warm instances (we need to justify reserved spend internally)?
3) Throttling/fallback: when you say "lower-cost model variant", do you mean an internally hosted smaller model or a Redwood-managed quantized fallback? How do you guarantee output quality / deterministic fallbacks?

Helios peak distribution: most spikes are bursty for ~6–8 minutes and account for ~18% of daily token volume. If we can cover 12–18 minute windows reliably, that would be fine.

One more ask: procurement needs the billing treatment spelled out (how credits are drawn down, overage unit rates, and an example monthly invoice). Can your team include a sample invoice?

—Emma

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>

Subject: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Thanks again for taking the demo today, Oliver — really appreciated the candid discussion. I wanted to capture the performance items we agreed to verify and call out the remaining gating questions so we have a clean MAP (mutual action plan).

High-level asks (from Brightbeam):
- 95th P50 latency target for typical single-turn inference: <= 120ms (text-gen, 512 tokens)
- Sustained throughput: 200 qps peak for batch endpoint, burst to 400 qps for short windows
- Concurrent live sessions for streaming use-case: target 120 concurrent streams with sub-200ms first-byte latency
- KV/prefix caching must be supported for repeated user context (expected 70% reuse)

What we (Redwood) showed and next validation steps:
- Demo highlights: streaming demo with client-side latency ~170ms median (recorded), autoscaling policy spun an additional worker at +200 qps
- Action: Share sample perf logs + demo recording (attached).
- Action: Run a short pilot with production-ish traces (we requested their representative workload — Brightbeam to provide sample traces by EOD Tue).

Open technical gating questions for Brightbeam to confirm:
1) Exact token distribution for the 512-token scenario (how often do they hit 512 vs 128 vs 32?)
2) Allowed tail latency for first-byte on streaming (is 200ms strict or a guideline?)
3) Data residency / audit requirements for the pilot (which region + retention)

Proposed timeline (subject to your trace delivery):
- Tue (01/10): Brightbeam shares anonymized traces + sample payloads
- Wed–Thu: Redwood runs pilot (2 days) and sends perf artifact (logs + replay summary)
- Fri: Joint call to review SLO checklist and decision criteria

Owners:
- Aisha Rahman (Redwood AE) — commercial & timeline
- Camila Reyes (SE) — pilot config and validation
- Marcus Lin (Solutions Eng) — log analysis and topology suggestions

Attachments: demo-recording-20280109.mp4 (30m), perf-logs-sample.json.gz, proposed_slo_checklist.xlsx

Short ask: Please confirm token distribution and whether 95th-latency at <= 120ms is a hard requirement or negotiable for a 30-day SLA. If you can share the traces tomorrow we’ll prioritize the pilot.

Thanks —
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123

Redwood Inference — Production-first inference for LLMs

Subject: Re: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Thanks, Aisha — great recap and sensible plan. A few clarifications from our side after internal sync: we can provide representative traces by EOD tomorrow (they will be anonymized). Quick answers to your gating questions below.

1) Token distribution: roughly 60% are short queries (~16–48 tokens), 30% are mid (~128 tokens), 10% hit 512 tokens. We can surface a small sample with the traces.
2) Streaming first-byte latency: 200ms is our target, but we can be flexible to 250ms during pilot to allow for environment variance. For GA we’d like to tighten to <=200ms.
3) Data residency: pilot can run in us-west-2. We need audit logs for request/response hashes retained for 90 days; full payloads cannot be stored by Redwood unless explicitly allowed — hashed evidence is fine.

Two additional asks:
- Can you confirm TLS 1.2+ and KMS integration for keys used in private deployments?
- We have a standard security addendum — I’ve CC’d contracts. Priya will follow up if we need a custom PO.

We’re aligned on the timeline; we’ll send traces tomorrow and would like the pilot replay results by Thursday. Also please share the runbook / checklist you’ll use so our SRE can follow the same verification steps.

Oliver Grant
Platform Engineering Lead, Brightbeam Health
oliver.grant@brightbeam.com | +1 (617) 555-0987


From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@brightbeam.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Jan 2028 08:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Thanks Oliver — that token distribution is really helpful. Quick technical notes from our side ahead of running the pilot: 

- Pilot config: we’ll run two modes (1) replay of provided traces against your baseline model hosted on Redwood Dedicated, and (2) a streaming emulation with 120 concurrent streams for 30 minutes to observe first-byte and sustained tail behavior.
- Autoscaling policy: we’ll use a scale threshold of 180 qps sustained for 60s with a 3-worker max surge for burst windows. We’ll share exact instance/worker types in the runbook.
- Caching: our prefix KV caching can be enabled; based on your 70% reuse estimate we expect ~40% token-cost reduction in replay. We’ll include cache hit breakdown in the perf artifact.
- Security: TLS 1.2+ supported; KMS integrations available. For the pilot we will only store hashed request identifiers and aggregated metrics (no plaintext payloads) per your note.

Deliverables we’ll provide after the 48h run:
- Replay perf logs + percentile breakdowns (p50/p95/p99)
- Streaming session traces with first-byte and payload latencies
- Cache hit stats and token-cost delta estimate
- Recommended config for GA (batching/quantization suggestions)

SRE runbook (short):
- Step 1: Upload traces to shared Drive folder (we’ll send link)
- Step 2: Redwood runs replay + streaming emulation in us-west-2
- Step 3: Camila reviews raw logs with Marcus and posts summary to HubSpot record
- Step 4: Joint review call to go over acceptance criteria (we propose Fri 01/12 10:00 PT)

Attaching a concise checklist (slo-checklist.xlsx) with the exact measurement windows we’ll use.

—Camila Reyes
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>

Cc: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, contracts@brightbeam.com
Date: Thu, 12 Jan 2028 16:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-demo: SLO action matrix & gating questions

Perfect — thanks for the fast turnaround and clarifications, Oliver. Confirming the MAP and owners below (final items before pilot kickoff).

Pilot kickoff checklist (owners & due dates):
- Brightbeam: deliver anonymized traces and token distribution samples (Oliver / Priya) — due Tue 01/10 EOD (noted you’ll deliver tomorrow)
- Redwood: run 48h replay + 30m streaming emulation in us-west-2 (Camila / Marcus) — Wed–Thu 01/11–01/12
- Redwood: deliver perf artifact (logs + summary) and cost delta estimate (Camila) — Thu 01/12 by 18:00 PT
- Joint review: Fri 01/13 10:00 PT (proposed) — confirm attendee list
- Contracts: Priya / contracts@brightbeam.com to share any SAs or SDA items; Aisha to coordinate commercial terms and PO (Aisha)

Decision criteria we’ll use on review call:
- Functional: p95 <= 120–250ms (pilot tolerance window: 250ms); p99 visibility and acceptable rollback plan if p99 exceeds threshold by >2x
- Throughput: sustain 200 qps with ability to burst to 400 qps for at least 1–2 minute windows without request failures >0.1%
- Streaming: first-byte median <200ms, pilot allowance to 250ms; must show stable connection handoff under 120 concurrent streams
- Security/compliance: hashed request evidence + 90-day audit log retention and TLS 1.2+/KMS confirmation

Next steps (today): I’ll upload the runbook + demo recording to the shared Drive and create the HubSpot task (link included). Marcus — please prepare any topology diagrams you want me to include in the shared folder.

If this all looks good I’ll ping Priya to begin the contracts thread and we’ll target the joint review on Friday.

Cheers,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123

From: Asha Rao <asha.rao@luminahealth.com>

Cc: Procurement Ops <procurement@luminahealth.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Jan 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Order Form: Redwood - Lumina Health (pricing schedule & tax handling)

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the runbook and the draft order form. Before we can route to procurement I need a couple of clarifications from your side so we can attach clean docs to our PO:

- Please confirm the invoicing entity (we have Redwood Inference, Redwood Labs, etc) and which entity will issue the invoice we should remit to.
- Net terms: Lumina prefers Net 60 but we can flex. Can you confirm Redwood's acceptable net terms and whether you can support Net 45 for this volume?
- Taxes/VAT: we need explicit language about which party is responsible for VAT/GST and an invoice flow showing VAT line item. Also — Lumina requires a tax residency certificate for less-common jurisdictions.
- Minimum commit cadence: Can you split the annual commit into quarterly tranches for invoicing/proration? Our finance team needs the schedule spelled out.

If you can send a redline that incorporates those items (and flag any non-starters from legal), we can move to PO and procurement approval this week.

Thanks,
Asha Rao
Procurement Lead — Lumina Health
asha.rao@luminahealth.com
(415) 555-0198

Hi Asha —

Attached is our updated order form redline (orderform_redline_v2.docx) that addresses the items you listed. Quick summary of the substantive changes:

- Invoicing entity: Invoices will be sent from "Redwood Inference, Inc." with legal entity and remit-to details included in the invoice header. Please confirm this maps to Lumina's AP expectations.
- Net terms: We are able to offer Net 45 as a middle ground. I called out Net 60 as an exception path for enterprise procurement approval, but default will be Net 45.
- Taxes/VAT: Added a tax allocation clause (Section 5): Redwood will list VAT/GST line items when applicable. Contract-level responsibility remains with Lumina (customer) except where Redwood is required by local law to collect VAT; we've added a procedure for producing tax residency forms.
- Commit cadence: Split annual commit into four quarterly invoices with proration language for partial usage. See Exhibit A for schedule.

Marissa/Karthik — tagging you for legal/finance confirmation on the wording in Sections 3–6.

Next steps I propose: 1) legal reviews redline (Marissa), 2) Lumina confirms VAT docs needed (Asha), 3) Redwood issues consolidated final for signature.

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0112

Attachments: orderform_redline_v2.docx (docx), tax_evidence_schedule.pdf (pdf)


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 11 Jan 2028 08:42:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Order Form: Redwood - Lumina Health (pricing schedule & tax handling)

Ben —

Thanks for the redline. I reviewed orderform_redline_v2 and pushed legal edits (see attached redline_legal_v1.docx). High level:

- Indemnity: We need to tighten the mutual indemnity carve-outs for third-party IP and explicitly exclude damages arising from data provided by the customer. I drafted alternative language in Section 10 and left the original text in tracked changes.
- Audit/Logs: Lumina requested audit log retention details. We can confirm retention but need to add a maximum retention period and a deletion process for regulated PII — see proposed Section 7.2.
- Taxes: The tax allocation clause is OK in principle, but we should not accept open-ended tax liability where Redwood is not legally liable. I added a sentence requiring Lumina to provide a tax residency certificate (or equivalent) prior to Redwood issuing tax-exempt invoices.
- Signoff path: suggest we keep payment terms as Net 45 in the contract and document an exception path in the SOW/order form where Lumina's procurement can request Net 60. That keeps the master contract consistent.

Two items still open that I'd like internal alignment on before we send to Lumina: (1) the exact indemnity phrase (Ben/Karthik to confirm commercial tolerance), and (2) whether we will accept invoice routing to the Lumina AP mailbox without entity-level PO attachments (Kimberly, do we have a revenue ops preference?).

Subject: Re: Order Form: Redwood - Lumina Health (pricing schedule & tax handling)

Hi Asha / Jordan —

We appreciate the fast turnaround. We've incorporated Marissa's legal edits into a consolidated redline (orderform_consolidated_redline.docx) but there are two remaining commercial/contract items for Lumina to confirm:

title: ZenPath IAM evidence: SCIM delta, SAML samples & role mapping delivery
preview:
From: Elena Park <ela@zenpath.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: ZenPath Security <security@zenpath.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Jan 2028 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: SCIM/SAML/Role artifacts for SOC 2 / SIG intake

Hi Ben —

Following our intake call last week, the ZenPath security team needs a small bundle of IAM artifacts so our auditor (SigmaAudit) can validate account lifecycle and RBAC controls for the SOC 2 / SIG review. We want the minimal set to avoid PII exposure; please see our ask below and confirm whether SFTP or Drive is easier for you: 

Required (examples and date range):
- SCIM delta export (CSV) covering 2027-12-01 -> 2027-12-31 (include headers + sample of changed attributes).
- 3 SAML assertion samples for interactive login (include timestamps and NameID formats).
- Role-to-entitlement mapping (CSV) for tenant zenpath-prod and a small sample of role assignments.
- Complete inventory of service accounts and delegated permissions (identify any cross-tenant/third-party connectors).
- Any documented least-privilege exceptions + short justification memo.

Deliverables should be sanitized: redact emails where required, remove tokens/keys, and provide a manifest (sha256) for integrity checks. If you need a partial delivery (sampling) we can accept ~10% SCIM sampling + full role map.

Can you confirm a target delivery date and preferred transfer method? We have SigmaAudit on standby and will provide a secure intake mailbox.

Thanks,
Elena Park
Security Program Manager — ZenPath
ela@zenpath.com

Attachments: intake_checklist.pdf (referenced)

To: Elena Park <ela@zenpath.com>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Jan 2028 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: SCIM/SAML/Role artifacts for SOC 2 / SIG intake

Elena —

Two quick clarifying q's before we scope work: 
1) For the SCIM delta, do you want the full delta CSV for the month or a sampled subset (we can provide full if you accept a short HMAC/manifest requirement)?
2) For SAML assertions, we will provide three anonymized assertion objects (no raw user emails; we can replace with hashed IDs) — is that acceptable to SigmaAudit?

Proposed timeline: initial sanitized bundle by EOD Thu (2028-01-12) with a final locked package by Tue (2028-01-17) if that aligns with your intake window. If you need faster, we can move the sample to Wed with a minimal set.

Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference

Ben / Marcus —

Plan below. I will own artifact curation and redaction for the bundle. High-level:

What we'll produce (sanitized):
- zenpath_scim_delta_sample_202712.csv — SCIM delta extracted from the provisioning service; will include attributes: user.externalId, user.active, groups (when available), lastModified, and changeType. We will deliver a 15% random sample by default unless ZenPath requests full month.
- zenpath_saml_assertions_samples.json — three assertion objects with NameID replaced by krb-hash-<short> and timestamps preserved; no raw email addresses.
- zenpath_role_mapping_202712.csv — canonical role -> entitlement mapping for tenant zenpath-prod (full export).
- zenpath_service_account_inventory.xlsx — inventory with owner/team column; service-account keys/credentials will be redacted (column marked REDACTED).
- artifact_manifest_sha256.txt — sha256 for each file listed.

Transfer and access: SFTP is preferred. We will stage files to sftp.redwood.secure/zenpath-intake/ (temporary creds to be created). If ZenPath prefers Drive, we will place them at: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1A2b3ZenPathEvidence (link provided for reviewer convenience).

Redaction rules:
- Replace user emails with HMAC-SHA256(email) truncated to 12 chars (we will provide mapping only to SigmaAudit under NDA).
- Remove any token/secret fields and replace with "<REDACTED>" literal.
- For service account keys we will only include metadata (key id, creation date) and mark the secret columns REDACTED.

Estimated effort: 6–8 engineer-hours to collect + 2 hours for redaction and manifesting. Target delivery to Elena (and SigmaAudit intake) by Thu 2028-01-12 17:00 PST.

Irene Choi
Security & Compliance Manager | Redwood Inference
irene@redwood.com

preview:
From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@medinovahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@medinovahealth.com
Date: Fri, 10 Dec 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Live preview request + procurement checklist

Hi Ben —

Thanks again for the product overview yesterday. The execs liked the flow and we'd like to schedule a short 30–45 minute live preview next week so our SRE and procurement folks can see behavior under live load.

Quick heads up from procurement: before a live demo they require a completed vendor security questionnaire and at least a SOC2 summary / policies doc. They also sent our standard NDA back with a few clauses redlined (attached).

Can you confirm:
- Whether Redwood can complete the attached questionnaire in advance (we're ideally looking for answers within 3 business days)
- If you can share SOC2 summary and where we can point our security team for evidence

If procurement won't sign off quickly we'll likely ask to move the live preview out — happy to take an async recording in the meantime.

Best,
Elena Rossi
Director, Vendor Programs
Medinova Health

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@medinovahealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Dec 2027 11:07:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Live preview request + procurement checklist

Elena — thanks for the clear note and the files. We can absolutely be responsive here. A few points: 

- We'll start populating the questionnaire today and aim to return a draft within 48 hours.
- I'll attach a short SOC2 summary and a runbook for data handling (see attached Redwood_PSC-SOC2-Summary.pdf). For full reports we can follow your secure transfer/process.
- Regarding the NDA redlines: I looked through them and there are three clauses that will need legal review on our side (mainly scope of liability and data deletion timelines). I can send a redlined response by EOD Monday.

Options for the demo while procurement finishes their review:
1) Hold the live preview until procurement confirms (preferred for SRE deep-dive).
2) Run a limited live preview that excludes PII-related flows and call it a technical walkthrough.
3) Send a 20–30 minute recorded walkthrough now and reconvene for live once paperwork is closed.

Which would your team prefer? If you want option 2 we can provision a restricted demo environment and lock off certain features quickly.

Ben

Mobile: (415) 555-0192

From: Sarah Montgomery <sarah.montgomery@acmehealth.com>

Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@acmehealth.com>, Kevin Lowe <kevin@barristerllp.com>
Date: Fri, 03 Dec 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: MSA redlines & GDPR: SCCs, roles, and transfer-impact evidence

Hi Avery,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Legal has reviewed the MSA + Order Form and pushed back on a few items tied to the Data Processing Addendum (we've also flagged SCC adherence and processor/controller roles). To keep timelines tight for our Q1 rollout, we need to lock the following by end of next week:

- Confirmation of controller/processor allocation in the DPA (section 2) — client counsel wants explicit language for joint processing scenarios.
- SCCs: confirmation which SCC set Redwood intends to rely on and whether you accept our proposed vendor clause.
- Transfer-impact addendum: we need a short schedule describing where data will transit and which regions are covered.
- Subprocessor list + KMS options (we need a draft E2E diagram or a short note).

Attaching our redline (acme_msa_redline_v3.docx) for your legal team. Can we get a single consolidated response with proposed alternative wording and a short mutual action plan for approvals? Also copying our outside counsel Kevin.

Best,
Sarah
Sarah Montgomery
Head of Procurement, Acme Health
Attachment: acme_msa_redline_v3.docx (docx)


From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sarah Montgomery <sarah.montgomery@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Lowe <kevin@barristerllp.com>
Date: Mon, 06 Dec 2027 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MSA redlines & GDPR: SCCs, roles, and transfer-impact evidence

Hi Sarah — thanks for sending the redline and the clear checklist. We can meet the timeline but want to coordinate internally to produce a consolidated redline and the transfer-impact summary. Proposed plan:

1) Ben will own the DPA and SCC responses (he'll produce a short memo with suggested rewording for the roles section and the SCC clause by EOD Wed).
2) Laura will review any pricing / SLA knock-on effects for Dedicated/Private deployment that could appear in the Order Form.
3) We'll provide a subprocessor inventory and a draft transfer-impact addendum (1 page) plus KMS options and a diagram.

Requests from our side to be efficient:
- Can you confirm whether your legal counsel requires the 2021 EU Commission SCCs or the updated standard contractual clauses published 2024?
- Please share your finalized subprocessor questionnaire (if different from the CSV you previously shared) so Ben can map responsibilities.

I'll kick off an internal sync now and loop in Ben. Expect our consolidated response no later than Thu, 09 Dec 2027. If that timing won't work on your end, let us know.

— Avery
Avery Johnson
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference | av ery.johnson@redwoodinference.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Kevin Lowe <kevin@barristerllp.com>, Sarah Montgomery <sarah.montgomery@acmehealth.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 08 Dec 2027 08:08:00 -0800
Subject: Draft DPA responses + proposed language (roles, SCCs, transfer addendum)

Kevin / Sarah — attaching a short redline summary and a recommended set of edits (redline_notes_acme_dpa.docx). High-level answers and asks below to keep everything in one place:

1) Controller / Processor roles
- Proposed: keep MSA section granting Redwood processor status for customer data processing, with an explicit carveout that Acme remains controller for patient-identifiable records. Proposed text: "For clarity, Acme Health is Controller for Personal Data it collects in connection with patient care; Redwood acts as Processor for processing performed on behalf of Acme pursuant to the Order Form."
- If Acme requests joint-controller language, can you confirm the specific obligations you expect in practice (data subject requests, retention commitments)? That affects operational commitments.

2) SCCs
- Redwood intends to rely on the EU-2024 SCCs (we updated our templates in July 2027). We are willing to adopt your custom vendor clause provided it does not expand liability beyond the MSA caps. Proposed compromise in clause 9.3: accept vendor clause subject to mutual indemnity carveouts — see redline.

3) Transfer-impact addendum
- We propose a 1-page schedule listing: primary hosting (us-west-2, us-east-1), disaster recovery backups (eu-west-1), and limited analytics routing to us-central for model training. We'll include a short description of pseudonymization/aggregation steps.

4) Subprocessor / KMS evidence
- Please send the most recent subprocessor CSV. We will attach our public subprocessor registry and the KMS options (customer-managed key available under Dedicated/Private plans).

Planned deliverables by Thu 09 Dec EOD:
- Consolidated DPA redline (docx)
- 1-page transfer-impact addendum (pdf)
- Subprocessor reconciliation (csv)
- Short operational note on data subject request workflow for joint-controller scenarios (if requested)

Attachment: redline_notes_acme_dpa.docx (docx)

If you prefer, we can run a 30-min legal-to-legal call Thu morning.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Director, Contracts & Privacy Operations, Redwood Inference

- We propose a 1-page schedule listing: primary hosting (us-west-2, us-east-1), disaster recovery backups (eu-west-1), and limited analytics routing to us-central for model training. We'll include a short description of pseudonymization/aggregation steps.

title: Adapter stability and partner SLAs for observability connectors
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Alice Chen <alice@datadog.com>, Patrick O'Neil <patrick@grafana.com>, Ravi Sharma <ravi@observify.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 03 Dec 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Adapter stability and partner SLAs for observability connectors

Hi all,

Following up from last week's sync — we want to formalize an \"adapter stability\" set of expectations tied to partner integrations (Datadog, Grafana, Observify). The goal is to reduce customer incidents and make upgrades predictable. Attached is a concise test plan (adapter-test-plan.xlsx) that outlines smoke checks we can run in partner CI.

High-level proposal: 
- Compatibility window: partners commit to support for N+2 minor versions of their primary agent/SDKs for 12 months.
- Breaking-change notice: minimum 30 days for minor compatibility breaks, 90 days for major breaking changes.
- Production smoke tests: Redwood will provide a harness (see sheet 2) — we ask partners to run it against staging weekly and share results.
- Escalation path: define 24/7 on-call contact for incidents impacting production connector stability.

Can you review and confirm any conflicts with your release cadence or legal/commercial constraints? Specific asks:
1) Partner provides a stable contact for escalation and a brief runbook for common failures.
2) Partners confirm they can run the attached test harness or we schedule a short onboarding call.
3) If you need different notice windows, flag them now so we can capture in the SLA.

Thanks — I want to lock the MAP by Dec 15 so we can include SLA language in the upcoming SOW.

Best,
Ben Carter
Head of Partnerships, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

Attachment: adapter-test-plan.xlsx (sheet list + test descriptions)

From: Alice Chen <alice@datadog.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 03 Dec 2027 12:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Adapter stability and partner SLAs for observability connectors

Ben — thanks for the clear write-up and the test plan. A few quick notes from Datadog's side: 

1) Release alignment: Datadog does monthly agent releases; our internal SLA team prefers a compatibility window that maps to two agent releases (approx ~8 weeks) rather than calendar months. We're ok with the spirit of N+2 if we map it to our release count.

2) Automated smoke endpoint: We can expose a lightweight smoke endpoint for partners if Redwood's harness hits an authenticated endpoint (we'd prefer token-based calls from Redwood CI).

3) Breaking-change notice: 30 days for behavioral and 90 days for API schema changes is reasonable. For agent-level deprecations, we usually publish a deprecation notice at least 60 days prior to the release — we can extend to 90 if needed for customers on long-term support.

4) Test execution: Datadog can run the harness in our CI, but we'll need a short onboarding doc and a sample dataset to validate. Happy to schedule 45m next week to walk through the harness.

Could we add a short line in the SLA about data retention for test runs (we typically purge ephemeral test payloads after 7 days)?

Alice
Alice Chen
Technical Partner Manager, Datadog
alice@datadog.com

> On Fri, Dec 3, 2027 at 9:12 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Following up from last week's sync — we want to formalize an \"adapter stability\" set of expectations...


From: Patrick O'Neil <patrick@grafana.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Alice Chen <alice@datadog.com>, Ravi Sharma <ravi@observify.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 04 Dec 2027 08:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Adapter stability and partner SLAs for observability connectors

Ben / team — appreciate the initiative. A couple items specific to Grafana plugin/connector lifecycles: 

- Versioning semantics: Grafana distinguishes breaking API/ABI changes (major) from connector surface-area changes (minor). We recommend the SLA call out semantics (major vs minor) and the expected team response time for each.
- Plugin marketplace: If we need to publish a Grafana plugin update to the marketplace, that can add a 3–5 day publishing lag — please factor that into notice windows for breaking changes.
- Compatibility matrix: we'd like Redwood to maintain a public compatibility matrix that maps Redwood runtime versions to supported Grafana plugin versions to reduce field confusion.

We can support weekly test runs from our CI and provide a staging Grafana instance for smoke tests, but we need the test harness to support token-based auth and a retry strategy (we throttle requests on staging).

Happy to jump on the onboarding call — next Wednesday afternoon my time works.

Patrick O'Neil
Partner Engineering, Grafana Labs
patrick@grafana.com

> On Fri, Dec 3, 2027 at 9:12 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Following up from last week's sync — we want to formalize an \"adapter stability\" set of expectations...


From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 06 Dec 2027 10:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Adapter stability and partner SLAs for observability connectors

Ben — looping in legal/compliance and ops notes here. I attached a draft SLA (SLA_draft_v2.pdf) with the following items we need to resolve before sign-offs: 

Key legal/ops asks:
- Liability cap language around third-party connector outages (we should propose a carve-out for partner-managed failures).
- Data handling for test payloads: confirm retention and whether partners can access test logs; we should require ephemerality and limited retention.
- Escalation SLA: 24x7 P1 contact is heavy — propose 8x5 with an on-call rotation for P0s only; partners should confirm if 24x7 is acceptable.
- Fallback behavior: outline graceful degradation — if a connector fails, traffic is buffered for up to 48 hours and then retried; provide a manual failover to a Redwood-owned ingestion path for critical customers.

Operational items:
- Compatibility matrix cadence ownership (we should decide if Redwood maintains this or partners keep their published matrix).
- Test harness onboarding: propose a 2-week window for initial onboarding, then weekly automated runs.

Action items (quick):
- Neha: draft the MAP with dates and owners (please include the HubSpot record and DocuSign link).
- Karthik: technical owner to run the first round of tests against partner staging.
- Ben: confirm commercial constraints (notice windows, marketplace publishing delays).

Laura Bennett
VP, Product Operations, Redwood Inference
laura@redwood.com

Attachment: SLA_draft_v2.pdf (summary + redlines)

Date: Tue, 07 Dec 2027 16:45:00 -0800

Thanks everyone — summary and proposed mutual action plan (MAP) below. Please reply with availability and sign-off items by EOD Dec 13 so we can lock the SOW. Links and artifacts are below.

MAP (proposed):
1) Week 0 (by Dec 15): Sign-off on SLA language (Laura to confirm legal carve-outs).
2) Week 1 (by Dec 22): Partners run initial adapter-test-plan.xlsx against staging; Karthik will coordinate.
3) Week 3 (by Jan 10): Automated weekly smoke tests enabled in partner CI with shared dashboards; partners to provide access tokens.
4) Week 6 (by Feb 1): Finalized compatibility matrix published (owner: partner + Redwood reviewer).

Owners & asks:
- Datadog (Alice): provide test-token and run harness against staging; confirm mapping of N+2 to agent releases.
- Grafana (Patrick): confirm marketplace publishing lag and token onboarding steps; provide a staging instance URL.
- Observify (Ravi): confirm escalation contact and on-call window.
- Redwood (Karthik): run first automated run and report results to MAP channel.
- Redwood (Laura): finalize SLA redlines and propose exact legal wording for partner failures.

Links / sign-offs: 
- HubSpot record: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/12345/company/88712
- DocuSign (SLA envelope): https://www.docusign.com/agreements/AX-99231
- Adapter test plan (attached): adapter-test-plan.xlsx

Please reply with any hard no's on the timeline or different notice windows. If OK, we'll schedule a 45m onboarding call next Wednesday to walk through the harness and token exchange.

Thanks — I will draft a one‑page MAP doc after any edits and circulate for signature.

Neha Kapoor
Senior Partner Programs Manager, Redwood Inference
neha@redwood.com

title: Proof-of-adoption gates and validation scenarios for Nimbus trial
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jen Simmons <jen.simmons@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 3 Dec 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Draft MAP + validation scenarios for the 30-day trial

Hi Jen —

Great to speak earlier. Per our call I put together a short mutual action plan and a candidate set of validation scenarios we think will demonstrate product-market fit for the 30-day trial. High level: we want to make acceptance binary and measurable so your evaluation team can make a confident go/no-go decision.

Attached: redwood_map_draft_v1.xlsx — outlines milestones, owners, and acceptance tests.

Key ask from you: confirm the 3 personas who will run the tests (clinical reviewer, integration engineer, ops lead) and confirm preferred weekly check-in cadence.

Suggested acceptance signals (first pass):
- Functional: 95% concordance on 3 labeled clinical prompt sets (see sheet ‘Eval sets’).
- Performance: 90th percentile end-to-end latency < 300ms for target payloads during your baseline traffic window.
- Operational: KV cache warms and reduces token cost by >= 15% in steady-state.

Also included are two exit gates we recommend for cutover: SOC2 evidence delivered (or in-flight with remediation plan), and a signed data processing addendum.

If helpful I can add a smoke-test runner script and sample postman collection for your engineers.

Thanks — looking forward to your feedback.

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0148

Attachment: redwood_map_draft_v1.xlsx (spreadsheet)


From: Jen Simmons <jen.simmons@nimbushealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Tim Rojas <tim.rojas@nimbushealth.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 3 Dec 2027 11:06:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Draft MAP + validation scenarios for the 30-day trial

Ben — thanks for this. Tim and I reviewed and this looks like the right direction. A few clarifying notes from the Nimbus side:

1) Personas: Clinical reviewer = Dr. Jen Patel (jen.patel@nimbushealth.com), Integration = Tim Rojas (tim.rojas@nimbushealth.com), Ops = Lauren Meyers (lauren.meyers@nimbushealth.com). Can you add them to the MAP owners?
2) We need a sharper definition for ‘concordance’ — is that token-level match or outcome-level? For our regulatory reviewers we need outcome-level agreement at the annotation label level.
3) Security: we can’t accept SOC2 ‘in-flight’ as a gate for production; for the pilot we will accept a completed questionnaire and a planned remediation timeline if there are gaps. I attached the questionnaire we typically use.

We propose weekly syncs on Wednesdays at 10am PT. Also — can you add a set of smoke tests that show failure modes (rate-limiting, degraded quantized model fallback)? Our SRE will run those.

Jen Simmons
Head of Product Operations, Nimbus Health
jen.simmons@nimbushealth.com

Attachment: nimbus_security_questionnaire.docx (doc)

Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Dec 2027 08:41:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Draft MAP + validation scenarios for the 30-day trial

Thanks Jen — great feedback. I updated the MAP and added the personas and cadence. A couple of points we clarified on our end:

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jen Simmons <jen.simmons@nimbushealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Dec 2027 09:04:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Draft MAP + validation scenarios for the 30-day trial

Jen / Team — quick note from an evaluation / close-readiness perspective. I added a short checkout checklist to the MAP and a proposed acceptance-test harness. Highlights: 

- Acceptance tests should be runnable end-to-end as a single job that outputs a pass/fail artifact. That artifact must include: run_id, dataset version, seed, and the F1 / confusion matrix CSV.
- Exit gate: at least two consecutive daily runs that meet pass thresholds, or a single corrective action with defined rollback points documented in the MAP.
- Edge-case sampling: 100 items from production-like traffic (anonymized) for manual review, flagged if concordance < 90% on any subpopulation.
- Performance SLA test: synthetic traffic generator that ramps to 2x expected pilot QPS for 10 minutes — 95th percentile latency must remain < 450ms, and fallback to quantized variant should not increase error by more than 3 percentage points.

I’ll own the acceptance-test harness and upload the runner to the Jira ticket once Ben creates it. Happy to iterate on the pass/fail artifact format if your analytics team has a preference.

Marissa Cole

title: Quick follow-up: Redwood demo + deployment fit
preview:
From: Carmen Lee <carmen.lee@nextech.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Patel <samir.patel@nextech.ai>
Date: 2027-12-05T16:12:00-08:00
Subject: Redwood demo follow-up + a few clarifying Qs

Thanks again for the demo today — quick note while things are fresh. NexTech's engineering lead (Samir) and I discussed the three deployment modes you outlined (Hosted API, Dedicated reserved capacity, Private VPC/on-prem). A few items we need clarified before we can lock in a short trial: 

- Expected QPS for a hosted eval: we want to simulate production burst patterns (10–20 qps sustained with spikes to 80 qps for 2–5 minutes). What rate limits and burst allowances would you expect during a hosted trial?
- Endpoint strategy: can we get separate endpoints per environment (staging/prod) and per model variant, or is routing done via API key + model param?
- Data handling: any persistence or logs kept by Redwood during hosted eval? We need confirmation on log retention and redaction options.
- Quickstart access: our infra team will need a simple runbook/endpoint list, and ideally a sandbox API key with predictable limits.

Also attaching our SOW template and a small network diagram showing that we may want Private later for compliance. Timeline: we want to run an initial hosted trial next week (start 2027-12-08) if we can get the sandbox access + ratecap agreed. Can you confirm?

Thanks,
Carmen
Carmen Lee
Director of Platform Product
NexTech Analytics

Attachment(s): NexTech_SOW_template.pdf, network_diagram.png


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
To: Carmen Lee <carmen.lee@nextech.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-12-05T18:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Redwood demo follow-up + a few clarifying Qs

Carmen — really appreciate the thoughtful write-up and the timeline. Quick recap and answers below; I also included Ben (AE) and Evelyn (Solutions) to scope the trial and Jordan (Support) for the sandbox setup.

Summary answers (high level):
- Hosted eval rate limits: default sandbox policy is 50 qps sustained per org with burst to 120 qps for short durations; we can temporarily raise to 200 qps for the trial window if you share expected traffic windows.
- Endpoints: we provide named endpoints per environment (staging/prod) in Hosted mode. You receive separate API keys scoped to each endpoint; model variant routing is via the endpoint configuration so you can pin a model or swap behind the same URL.
- Data handling: for Hosted evals we persist logs for 30 days by default; we can enable request redaction and restrict logs to metadata only for trial accounts on request (Evelyn will outline options).
- Quickstart: we will provision a sandbox org, two endpoints (staging/prod), and a trial API key. Expect a one-page runbook (auth, endpoints, typical cURL samples) and a checklist from us by EOD tomorrow.

Next steps I propose:
1) Carmen — confirm trial start date (you suggested 2027-12-08).
2) Samir — please share a short traffic profile (expected sustained qps, peak durations, average token length) for the trial period; that will let us tailor the temporary rate raise.
3) Ben/Evelyn/Jordan — I'll kick off provisioning once we have the traffic profile and Carmen's confirmation.

I attached a short quickstart checklist we use internally. If it's helpful, Ben can draft an estimated cost for the trial configuration (so procurement knows potential spend).

Best,
Aditya
Aditya Rao
Senior Account Executive
Redwood Inference

Attachment(s): redwood-quickstart-checklist.docx


From: Samir Patel <samir.patel@nextech.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Carmen Lee <carmen.lee@nextech.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-12-06T09:48:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Redwood demo follow-up + a few clarifying Qs

Aditya/Carmen — quick traffic profile from our side (high level):
- Baseline staging: 12 qps, avg 18 tokens per request.
- Baseline prod expected ramp: 18–22 qps, avg 24 tokens.
- Burst pattern: occasional bursts up to ~75–90 qps for 3–6 minutes during batch inference windows.
- Latency target: p95 < 400ms for user-facing endpoints.
- Data notes: we will not send PHI during the trial but will want to confirm retention and redaction settings as Carmen noted.

We are happy to start the trial 2027-12-08. Please proceed with sandbox provisioning and temporary rate increase.

Thanks,
Samir
Samir Patel
Lead Software Engineer
NexTech Analytics

- Provisioning: Jordan will create a NexTech sandbox org, two endpoints (staging / prod), and API keys. Action owner: Jordan. ETA: 2027-12-07 5pm PT.
- Rate limits: we will set the sandbox to 50 qps sustained with a temporary trial window rule to allow 200 qps bursts for 10-minute windows across 2027-12-08 to 2027-12-15. Action owner: Ben.
- Data & logs: Evelyn will send a short doc outlining the 30-day default retention, redaction toggles, and an opt-in to limit logs to metadata only for trial accounts. Action owner: Evelyn. ETA: EOD 2027-12-07.
- Runbook & test plan: Aditya to deliver a one-page runbook + sample curl commands and a minimal test plan for Samir's team.
- Procurement: Ben will include an estimated trial cost sheet and an optional quote for Dedicated capacity if NexTech decides to pursue reserved capacity after the trial.

If anything above doesn't match your expectations, call it out and we'll adjust. Carmen — can you confirm a single Slack or email point we should use for urgent trial issues? Also, please confirm whether NexTech wants the logs-redaction option enabled for the trial (yes/no).

title: Horizon windowing + trace storage + dashboard bundle — pricing and commit terms
preview:
From: Alex Morgan <alex@tidewave.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@tidewave.ai
Date: 2027-12-01T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Observability bundle question — dashboard + long window traces

Hi Ben / Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo earlier. TideWave is evaluating the observability add-ons and we have a couple of configuration asks we need to price out before procurement signs off:

- We want the Advanced Dashboards bundle (real-time and historical), full tracing with 12-month retention, and a separate "hotpath" tier for the most recent 14 days at low latency.
- We need storage breakdowns (ingest vs cold archival), and an indicative per-GB/month and per-ingest-unit price.
- Procurement asks whether there is a discount for a 12-month prepaid commit and whether Redwood can include SLA credits tied to trace-query latency.

I've attached our internal observability requirements (tidewave-observability-reqs.docx). Can you share a simple SOW and a pricing spreadsheet so we can circulate internally? Our procurement target is to finalize vendor terms by 2027-12-10.

Best,
Alex
Alex Morgan
Head of Platform — TideWave AI
alex@tidewave.ai


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex@tidewave.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>, procurement@tidewave.ai
Date: 2027-12-01T11:38:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Observability bundle question — dashboard + long window traces

Alex —

Appreciate the clear asks and the doc. Attaching a draft SOW and a compact pricing workbook that separates: Advanced Dashboards (seat + query quota), Trace ingest (per-ingest-unit), and Trace storage (hotpath 14d at NVMe vs cold archival S3 tier). Highlights up front:

- Indicative list price: Advanced Dashboards $12k/mo (up to 25 seats + 1M queries), Trace ingest $0.015/ingest-unit, Trace storage hotpath $0.30/GB-mo, cold archival $0.03/GB-mo.
- 12-month prepay discount: we can offer 15% off the add-on list prices for a 12-month prepaid commitment at or above $150k ARR on add-ons.
- SLA: 99.9% dashboard query latency for hotpath; we can put latency SLOs into the SOW with a service credit construct (0.25% credit per 30 min breach up to a cap).

Irene from our CS/Reliability team is cc'd and will comment on technical storage options below. If your team wants a deeper cost model (e.g., segmenting traces by service and sampling tiers) we can run a quick sizing exercise given sample ingest volumes.

Next steps: if the ballpark is acceptable I can route a redline-friendly SOW and a clean quote you can attach to the procurement packet.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
(925) 555-0149


From: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex@tidewave.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, procurement@tidewave.ai
Date: 2027-12-02T08:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Observability bundle question — dashboard + long window traces

On 2027-12-01 11:38, Ben Carter wrote:
> Irene from our CS/Reliability team is cc'd and will comment on technical storage options below.

Alex — quick technical notes so procurement has the right knobs:

- We split trace costs into ingest (compute + transient storage) and storage (hot vs cold). For large-volume services it's often cheaper to: 1) sample at ingest for low-value spans, 2) keep full-fidelity hotpath for 14d, and 3) compress/encode older traces into a low-cost archive with indexed pointers for retrieval. That usually halves the effective storage cost for 12-month workflows.
- Dashboard compute for historical queries is metered differently (query compute vs visualization seats). If TideWave expects heavy cross-service ad-hoc analysis, consider adding a query quota block instead of paying purely per-seat.
- Security/compliance: we can surface audit logs and access controls into the SOW (SOC 2 evidence available).

I'll prepare a short appendix with recommended sampling profiles and an option that caps storage spend (auto-archive policy). Let me know if you can share a 1-week sample of trace volume (GB/day) and average spans/sec.

— Irene
Irene Choi
Senior Solutions Engineer — Redwood Inference


From: Alex Morgan <alex@tidewave.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@tidewave.ai, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-12-03T10:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Observability bundle question — dashboard + long window traces

Ben / Irene —

Thanks for the quick turnaround. The cost buckets look reasonable but procurement pushed back on the 15% prepay discount; their ask is 20% for a 12-month prepay. To help bridge the delta, TideWave is willing to:

- Commit to a 12-month add-on spend target of $180k if Redwood will agree to 20% off add-on list prices.
- Include Redwood in our upcoming marketplace launch notes (marketing blurb + logo/link) if we can get the 20% and the SLA language in the SOW.

Also, procurement asked whether Redwood can cap monthly variability on the storage line (e.g., a 10% overage cap or a fixed not-to-exceed for the first 6 months) while we ramp instrumentation.

If you can provide an update today we can aim to run terms through our legal review on Monday.

Best,
Alex


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex@tidewave.ai>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene_choi@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, procurement@tidewave.ai
Date: 2027-12-04T15:30:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Observability bundle question — dashboard + long window traces

Alex —

Quick note after internal alignment: we can close to your ask with the following package: 

- 18% off add-on list prices for a 12-month prepay at a $150k minimum commitment.
- If TideWave prepays $180k or greater we will honor 20% off. (So your proposal hits the 20% band if you lock to $180k.)
- Include SLOs for hotpath trace-query latency in the SOW; we'll add the service credit language.
- For the first 6 months we can also include an overage-smoothing clause (cap monthly storage overage charge to 10% above the forecasted line item; excess above that will be billed monthly).
- Redwood will provide a marketplace marketing blurb and logo usage rights conditional on a mutual marketing review clause in the SOW.

I've attached the updated SOW (TideWave-RW_SOW_v3.pdf) and the priced workbook (pricing-breakdown-20271201.xlsx). If this aligns with your procurement ask, please confirm and we'll send a redline-ready contract (and a DocuSign link) today.

Regards,
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
Attachments: TideWave-RW_SOW_v3.pdf; pricing-breakdown-20271201.xlsx

title: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lena Park <lena.park@cobaltsys.com>, Tom Harris <tharris@cobaltsys.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 30 Nov 2027 16:12:00 -0800
Subject: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff

Hi Lena / Tom —

As discussed during last week's kickoff, I'm moving the implementation handoff into motion for the Private deployment (VPC peering + private endpoints + on-prem package). Attached is the first cut of the integration playbook we will use as the single source of truth during the implementation. Key items included: a mutual action plan, required network artifacts, firewall / ACL expectations, on‑prem installer checklist, and a proposed staging schedule.

Attachments (attached to this thread):
- Cobalt-Integration-Playbook-v1.2.pdf (PDF, 42 pages)
- vpc-peering-template.yml (YAML, sample peering configuration)
- onprem-install-checklist.xlsx (Excel, installer runbook and OS/preflight checks)

Immediate asks:
- Can you confirm your preferred maintenance window for on‑prem install (weeknights vs weekend)?
- Please provide the IP range you plan to use for the private endpoints and whether you will NAT behind a firewall or allow direct routing to our private IPs.
- If you have an internal ticket or RFC for VPC peering, drop the ID and we'll align our network team.

Proposed next step: 60‑minute alignment call to walk the playbook and agree on the network cutover/test plan. I've tentatively proposed Fri 12/3 14:00 PST — if that works I will send the invite.

I'll be coordinating the customer success and implementation activities. Monica will own data prep and acceptance criteria; Priyom will be the lead for the runtime deployment (on‑prem agent & installer); Marcus is our network SME and will be on the peering/BGP items. Grace is the CS lead for escalation and success metrics.

Thanks — looking forward to getting this staged.

—
Avery Johnson
Customer Success Manager | Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com | +1 415 555 0123



From: Lena Park <lena.park@cobaltsys.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Tom Harris <tharris@cobaltsys.com>, eng-infra@cobaltsys.com
Date: Wed, 01 Dec 2027 09:23:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff

Thanks Avery —

Appreciate the clear playbook. Quick responses to your asks:
- Preferred maintenance window: weekend overnight (Sat 01:00–05:00 local) — lower traffic for our batch jobs.
- Private endpoints: we plan to allocate 10.20.32.0/24 for the private endpoints. We will NAT outgoing traffic through our perimeter firewall (no direct public routing).
- VPC peer ticket: INFRA-7821 (I'll invite the infra ticket into the thread).

A couple of questions after a quick skim:
1) For the on‑prem installer, does Redwood require inbound access from your control plane to the host, or can all communication be established outbound over TLS from our side?
2) The playbook references 'BGP ASN exchange' — are you expecting us to run BGP for failover, or is a static route + health probes sufficient for initial cutover?

We can jump on the proposed 12/3 slot; please send a calendar invite and a short agenda. Also — we need an updated SLA draft with the Private deployment metrics for our procurement team (I saw the DocSend link in related links; can you attach a redlined version for procurement?).

Cheers,
Lena Park
Infrastructure Program Manager
Cobalt Systems

On Tue, Nov 30, 2027 at 4:12 PM Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> [original message attached]



From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Lena Park <lena.park@cobaltsys.com>, Tom Harris <tharris@cobaltsys.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Dec 2027 11:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Cobalt Systems — Private Deployment Integration Playbook & Ops Handoff

Folks — looping in the infra details we need to finalize before the on‑prem installer run. Quick notes from the Redwood side: 

- Control plane connectivity: Our on‑prem agent establishes outbound TLS to our control plane; we do not require inbound open ports for the default installer flow. If Cobalt prefers an inbound management plane, we can support it but it requires an exception in the firewall (see playbook section 4.2).
- Private endpoints: 10.20.32.0/24 is fine; please confirm there are no overlapping prefixes with your staging environment.
- NAT vs direct routes: NAT outbound is supported; for KV/prefix cache efficiency we recommend direct private routing where possible but we'll work with NAT.
- BGP: For the initial cutover a static route + health probes is sufficient. If you want accelerated cross-region failover later, we recommend a later BGP step (we can run BGP peering with ASN exchange).

Attachment: vpc-peering-template.yml (sample BGP/static configuration) — we'll provide a filled example during the call.

Action items I propose before the call: 
1) Cobalt to confirm the exact host(s) where the on‑prem installer will run and provide OS images / kernel versions.
2) Cobalt to confirm whether the installer host will sit behind a NAT gateway.
3) Redwood to prepare two network diagrams (static-route flow; BGP flow) and one example peering config to share. Marcus will lead that.

Folks — looping in the infra details we need to finalize before the on‑prem installer run. Quick notes from the Redwood side: 

- Control plane connectivity: Our on‑prem agent establishes outbound TLS to our control plane; we do not require inbound open ports for the default installer flow. If Cobalt prefers an inbound management plane, we can support it but it requires an exception in the firewall (see playbook section 4.2).
- Private endpoints: 10.20.32.0/24 is fine; please confirm there are no overlapping prefixes with your staging environment.
- NAT vs direct routes: NAT outbound is supported; for KV/prefix cache efficiency we recommend direct private routing where possible but we'll work with NAT.
- BGP: For the initial cutover a static route + health probes is sufficient. If you want accelerated cross-region failover later, we recommend a later BGP step (we can run BGP peering with ASN exchange).

Attachment: vpc-peering-template.yml (sample BGP/static configuration) — we'll provide a filled example during the call.

Action items I propose before the call: 
1) Cobalt to confirm the exact host(s) where the on‑prem installer will run and provide OS images / kernel versions.
2) Cobalt to confirm whether the installer host will sit behind a NAT gateway.
3) Redwood to prepare two network diagrams (static-route flow; BGP flow) and one example peering config to share. Marcus will lead that.

I'll be ready to walk through the runtime install steps and failure remediation during the alignment call.
