title: HelioWorks billing: unexpected token spike and payment hold - request for ledger evidence
preview:
From: Sophie Lang <sophie@helioworks.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: HelioWorks Billing <billing@helioworks.io>
Date: Sun, 21 Nov 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Unexpected usage spike on Nov 1 - withholding payment pending ledger

Hi Ben,

We pulled the Nov invoice and saw a ~320% usage increase compared to October. We use Redwood heavily, but there was no prod change on our side that would account for this. We're putting the Nov payment on hold until we get itemized evidence for the spike (per-token attribution, request ids, and any sampling windows).

Attached is a small sample (usage-sample-20271120.csv) and our invoice (invoice-20271101.pdf). Can you confirm whether this looks like normal batch attribution or if there was a backfill/ingest reprocessor run on your side?

We'd like this resolved by EOD Monday; our procurement needs to clear the payment.

Thanks,
Sophie Lang
Head of Platform, HelioWorks
S: +1 (415) 555-0186

---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Lang <sophie@helioworks.io>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 22 Nov 2027 10:34:22 -0800
Subject: Re: Unexpected usage spike on Nov 1 - withholding payment pending ledger

Hi Sophie — thanks for the note and the attachments. I see the spike on our billing side as well and I understand the payment hold.

Quick status: I've pulled the preliminary telemetry and copied Kimberly (billing ops) to provide the detailed logs and a proposed remediation (credit/proration) if we determine this was on Redwood's side.

Can you confirm the following to speed triage:
- The account ID you used to query the CSV sample (helps match prefixes)
- Whether the spike manifests as many short streaming sessions (sub-1s tokens) or long chat sessions

I’ll aim to get you a first pass ledger export by EOD Tuesday. If we confirm a platform-side attribution issue we will propose a credit and unblock payment immediately.

Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (628) 555-0123

Attachment: usage-sample-20271120.csv (already received)

---

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Lang <sophie@helioworks.io>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 23 Nov 2027 08:15:09 -0800
Subject: Re: Unexpected usage spike on Nov 1 - withholding payment pending ledger

Hi Sophie — copying in more details below. I pulled the billable events and matched the CSV sample prefixes to our ingest stream. Preliminary findings:

1) Root cause hypothesis: a backfill job from HelioWorks' staging environment appears to have used a long-running API key that mapped to your production tenant for a 3-hour window on Nov 1 between 02:12–05:30 UTC. The calls are short-lived but extremely chatty (many small prompt/response cycles), which drives token counts up due to many round trips.

2) Evidence we have included:
- Token-level export (subset) for Nov 1: sample rows show repeated request_id values with sequential sub-requests.
- Router logs: a high burst of requests from an IP block you own (x.x.37.x) at the same times.
- No platform-side reprocessor/backfill runs scheduled on our side in that window; service-side maintenance logs show normal operation.

3) Suggested remediation:
- Provide HelioWorks a one-time prorated credit for the excess consumption that aligns to your October→November baseline (we calculated an approximate 72% credit on the delta above your 90th percentile historical daily usage).
- Recommend rotating the compromised API key and tagging staging keys to a separate tenant to avoid cross-environment attribution.

I can prepare the full CSV export (token, request_id, timestamp, model_variant, route) and a short reconciliation spreadsheet if you want to share a whitelisted S3 or Drive location.

If Helio wants to withhold payment beyond Nov 26 (close of month), please let us know and we'll escalate to leadership for expedited review.

Thanks,
Kimberly Park
Billing Operations Manager, Redwood Inference
kimberly.park@redwood.com

Attachments (proposed): token-export-nov1-sample.csv (text/csv); router-log-snippet.txt (text/plain)

---

From: HelioWorks Billing <billing@helioworks.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Nov 2027 09:22:41 -0800
Subject: Re: Unexpected usage spike on Nov 1 - withholding payment pending ledger

Ben / Kimberly —

Thanks for the quick look. We do have an internal incident that may have caused the staging key leak; we're running a root-cause and will rotate keys within 24 hours. Two clarifications before we accept the credit proposal:

1) We need the raw per-request request_id-level evidence (not just aggregated rows) for the full 3-hour window.
2) Procurement needs a formal statement from Redwood that there were no scheduled reprocessors/backfills during that period (to avoid audit headaches).

If you can provide those items we will unblock payment this week. If not, we have to keep the hold until Nov 30.

Regards,
Sophie (on behalf of HelioWorks Billing)

Attachment: invoice-20271101.pdf (application/pdf)

---

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Lang <sophie@helioworks.io>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 24 Nov 2027 16:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Unexpected usage spike on Nov 1 - withholding payment pending ledger

Sophie / HelioWorks team — looping in Marissa (Head of Revenue Operations) to sign the formal statement and approve the export delivery plan. Marissa, Kimberly can assemble the per-request CSV for the 02:12–05:30 UTC window and an ops-signed statement that no platform-run backfills/reprocessors were active then.

We propose:
- Token/request_id-level CSV delivered via their Drive folder by EOD Thursday (24 Nov 2027 PST).
- Marissa will sign the short statement regarding platform-side activity.
- Once Helio confirms receipt, Redwood will apply the prorated credit and lift the payment hold.

Marissa — can you confirm you can sign and send the statement?

Thanks all,
Ben

title: EU-only processing: segregation, backups and proof of erasure
preview:
From: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com
Date: 2027-11-21 09:12:00 +01:00
Subject: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week. As we scope a potential pilot, our security and privacy team has a few operational questions we need answered for our Schrems II review and SCCs mapping. High level: we need confirmation that inference and all persisted artifacts for NovaMed customers can be processed and stored exclusively in EU regions, and we need detail on backup retention/erasure.

Specific asks from our questionnaire (attached):
- Do backups/snapshots ever leave the EU region (including cold/archival copies)?
- How is operator access to in-region hosts and backups controlled and logged?
- Can you provide an example of the evidence Redwood can produce to show backups were purged (including snapshots/archives)?
- Is KMS key material for in-region storage customer-dedicated or multi-tenant?

We attached our SCCs questionnaire (Novamed_SCCs_questionnaire_v1.xlsx). Can you point us to a proposed timeline for providing operational evidence?

Appreciate a short response and suggested owners — happy to set a follow up call.

Best,
Julie Harrison
Head of Security Operations
NovaMed

Attachment: Novamed_SCCs_questionnaire_v1.xlsx


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-11-21 11:45:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Hi Julie,

Thanks — appreciate the clear asks and the attachment. Looping in Naomi (Privacy & Ops) and Lauren (Legal) who will own evidence and contractual language respectively.

Quick answers up front:
- Redwood can confine inference, persisted model artifacts, and customer data to EU regions for a scoped account; backup policies can be configured to remain in-region, and we support in-region archival options.
- Operator access is role-based with break-glass procedures; all actions are logged and retained in audit logs.
- We can provide deletion/erasure artifacts (audit entries, object delete manifests, snapshot destroy confirmation) — Naomi will outline the exact artefacts and timeline.
- KMS: we offer both tenant-dedicated and (managed) shared KMS options depending on your procurement and contract.

Attached is a short ops summary (redwood-eu-ops-summary.pdf) that covers our standard controls and what we typically produce for evidence. Proposed next step: Naomi will propose a granular evidence checklist and a two-week delivery window for the initial package. Lauren will draft contract language options.

If that works, I can schedule a 30-min sync mid-week.

Best,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood-eu-ops-summary.pdf


From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
To: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-11-22 14:02:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Julie, Ben —

Thanks, pulling together the checklist here. Below is a concise mapping of the artifacts and the operational steps we can provide for NovaMed to satisfy SCCs and Schrems II review points. We can produce the following for an in-scope account that elects EU-only processing: 

1) Data localization proof
- Account-level placement records (region tags on buckets/volumes) exported as CSV
- Network egress reports showing traffic confined to EU regions

2) Backups & archives
- Backup policy details (retention windows, replication targets). For EU-only: backups are configured to replicate to EU-only cold buckets; we can disable cross-region replication for the account.
- Evidence of deletion: object deletion manifests, snapshot destroy events, and underlying storage provider delete confirmations (where available). We retain delete audit logs for 12 months by default; we can extend retention on request as part of the contract.

3) Operator access controls
- RBAC + ephemeral access via JIT (just-in-time) sessions; all sessions require approval and are recorded.
- Privileged actions on in-region hosts and backup stores produce audit entries with user, action, timestamp, and justification.

4) KMS and key custody
- Customer-dedicated KMS keys can be used (separate key rings per customer); we can support customer-controlled keys via bring-your-own-key in supported clouds.

5) Subprocessors and export controls
- Subprocessors that handle backups or operator tooling are below — we will include location and SCCs mapping in the artifacts bundle.

Deliverables and timeline (proposed):
- Day 0–3: scoped configuration check (confirm account flags for EU-only processing)
- Day 4–10: extract technical evidence (region tags, backup policies, deletion manifests, audit logs)
- Day 11–14: compile evidence bundle + legal attestation language

Attachments: operator-access-mapping.xlsx (mapping of control owners and logs), data-erasure-playbook.pdf (example of deletion workflow and evidence types).

Notes and limitations:
- Cold/archival providers sometimes expose deletion proofs asynchronously; where full delete-confirmation APIs are unavailable we provide internal snapshot destroy records plus storage provider confirmations where possible.
- If NovaMed requires immutable contractual proof of erasure for historic backups older than X days, we recommend adding explicit SLA language and acceptance criteria to the DPA (Lauren can draft).

Happy to present this runbook on a call. If you want us to target a narrower list of artifacts (e.g., only snapshots + logs), tell us which items are highest priority.

Regards,
Naomi Feldman
Head of Privacy & Compliance, Redwood Inference

Attachments: operator-access-mapping.xlsx, data-erasure-playbook.pdf


From: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>
To: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com
Date: 2027-11-23 10:20:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Naomi — thanks, this is exactly the level we were hoping for. A couple of clarifications before we sync:

1) Backups older than 30 days: our legal team is asking for a reproducible artifact that demonstrates purge of archived backups (not just logs) where retention has been exceeded. Can Redwood provide a chain-of-custody style confirmation for archival deletes?

2) Subprocessor locations: can you provide a short list of any third-party vendors that would have potential access to backup media (e.g., managed storage, cold vaults)? We need region-level detail for each subprocessor.

If you can answer those two items in the evidence bundle, we can move to the contractual addendum phase. Also, please include any sample log extracts (timestamped) — that speeds our audit.

Best,
Julie

Attachment: (none)


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-11-24 16:05:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: EU-only processing: segregation, backups and proof of erasure

Julie —

Thanks for the clarifying points. From a contractual standpoint we can: 

- Add an EU-only processing addendum to the DPA that requires Redwood to maintain backups and archives within EU regions for the scoped account and to notify NovaMed before any change.
- Add an operational evidence SLA: Redwood will provide deletion artifacts (audit logs, snapshot destroy manifests, provider confirmations where available) within 10 business days of request for the prior 12 months. If NovaMed requires a longer historical window or immutable proof for archived media, we will propose a bespoke schedule and associated fees in the amendment.
- Include a subprocessor exhibit listing vendors and region-level locations; we will bind subprocessors to SCCs and provide the mapping.

Next steps: we will prepare (1) a draft DPA addendum and (2) the evidence bundle Naomi described. Ben — please confirm a delivery date for the evidence package (Naomi has proposed 14 days from scope confirmation). Julie — please confirm whether the 12-month window and 10 business day SLA meet Novamed's audit needs or if you require a different baseline.

text:
From: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com
Date: 2027-11-21 09:12:00 +01:00
Subject: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week. As we scope a potential pilot, our security and privacy team has a few operational questions we need answered for our Schrems II review and SCCs mapping. High level: we need confirmation that inference and all persisted artifacts for NovaMed customers can be processed and stored exclusively in EU regions, and we need detail on backup retention/erasure.

Specific asks from our questionnaire (attached):
- Do backups/snapshots ever leave the EU region (including cold/archival copies)?
- How is operator access to in-region hosts and backups controlled and logged?
- Can you provide an example of the evidence Redwood can produce to show backups were purged (including snapshots/archives)?
- Is KMS key material for in-region storage customer-dedicated or multi-tenant?

We attached our SCCs questionnaire (Novamed_SCCs_questionnaire_v1.xlsx). Can you point us to a proposed timeline for providing operational evidence?

Appreciate a short response and suggested owners — happy to set a follow up call.

Best,
Julie Harrison
Head of Security Operations
NovaMed

Attachment: Novamed_SCCs_questionnaire_v1.xlsx


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-11-21 11:45:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Hi Julie,

Thanks — appreciate the clear asks and the attachment. Looping in Naomi (Privacy & Ops) and Lauren (Legal) who will own evidence and contractual language respectively.

Quick answers up front:
- Redwood can confine inference, persisted model artifacts, and customer data to EU regions for a scoped account; backup policies can be configured to remain in-region, and we support in-region archival options.
- Operator access is role-based with break-glass procedures; all actions are logged and retained in audit logs.
- We can provide deletion/erasure artifacts (audit entries, object delete manifests, snapshot destroy confirmation) — Naomi will outline the exact artefacts and timeline.
- KMS: we offer both tenant-dedicated and (managed) shared KMS options depending on your procurement and contract.

Attached is a short ops summary (redwood-eu-ops-summary.pdf) that covers our standard controls and what we typically produce for evidence. Proposed next step: Naomi will propose a granular evidence checklist and a two-week delivery window for the initial package. Lauren will draft contract language options.

If that works, I can schedule a 30-min sync mid-week.

Best,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood-eu-ops-summary.pdf


From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
To: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-11-22 14:02:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Julie, Ben —

Thanks, pulling together the checklist here. Below is a concise mapping of the artifacts and the operational steps we can provide for NovaMed to satisfy SCCs and Schrems II review points. We can produce the following for an in-scope account that elects EU-only processing: 

1) Data localization proof
- Account-level placement records (region tags on buckets/volumes) exported as CSV
- Network egress reports showing traffic confined to EU regions

2) Backups & archives
- Backup policy details (retention windows, replication targets). For EU-only: backups are configured to replicate to EU-only cold buckets; we can disable cross-region replication for the account.
- Evidence of deletion: object deletion manifests, snapshot destroy events, and underlying storage provider delete confirmations (where available). We retain delete audit logs for 12 months by default; we can extend retention on request as part of the contract.

3) Operator access controls
- RBAC + ephemeral access via JIT (just-in-time) sessions; all sessions require approval and are recorded.
- Privileged actions on in-region hosts and backup stores produce audit entries with user, action, timestamp, and justification.

4) KMS and key custody
- Customer-dedicated KMS keys can be used (separate key rings per customer); we can support customer-controlled keys via bring-your-own-key in supported clouds.

5) Subprocessors and export controls
- Subprocessors that handle backups or operator tooling are below — we will include location and SCCs mapping in the artifacts bundle.

Deliverables and timeline (proposed):
- Day 0–3: scoped configuration check (confirm account flags for EU-only processing)
- Day 4–10: extract technical evidence (region tags, backup policies, deletion manifests, audit logs)
- Day 11–14: compile evidence bundle + legal attestation language

Attachments: operator-access-mapping.xlsx (mapping of control owners and logs), data-erasure-playbook.pdf (example of deletion workflow and evidence types).

Notes and limitations:
- Cold/archival providers sometimes expose deletion proofs asynchronously; where full delete-confirmation APIs are unavailable we provide internal snapshot destroy records plus storage provider confirmations where possible.
- If NovaMed requires immutable contractual proof of erasure for historic backups older than X days, we recommend adding explicit SLA language and acceptance criteria to the DPA (Lauren can draft).

Happy to present this runbook on a call. If you want us to target a narrower list of artifacts (e.g., only snapshots + logs), tell us which items are highest priority.

Regards,
Naomi Feldman
Head of Privacy & Compliance, Redwood Inference

Attachments: operator-access-mapping.xlsx, data-erasure-playbook.pdf


From: Julie Harrison <julie.harrison@novamed.com>
To: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@novamed.com
Date: 2027-11-23 10:20:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: EU-only processing: operational segregation & backups

Naomi — thanks, this is exactly the level we were hoping for. A couple of clarifications before we sync:

1) Backups older than 30 days: our legal team is asking for a reproducible artifact that demonstrates purge of archived backups (not just logs) where retention has been exceeded. Can Redwood provide a chain-of-custody style confirmation for archival deletes?

2) Subprocessor locations: can you provide a short list of any third-party vendors that would have potential access to backup media (e.g., managed storage, cold vaults)? We need region-level detail for each subprocessor.

If you can answer those two items in the evidence bundle, we can move to the contractual addendum phase. Also, please include any sample log extracts (timestamped) — that speeds our audit.

Best,
Julie

Julie —

Thanks for the clarifying points. From a contractual standpoint we can: 

- Add an EU-only processing addendum to the DPA that requires Redwood to maintain backups and archives within EU regions for the scoped account and to notify NovaMed before any change.
- Add an operational evidence SLA: Redwood will provide deletion artifacts (audit logs, snapshot destroy manifests, provider confirmations where available) within 10 business days of request for the prior 12 months. If NovaMed requires a longer historical window or immutable proof for archived media, we will propose a bespoke schedule and associated fees in the amendment.
- Include a subprocessor exhibit listing vendors and region-level locations; we will bind subprocessors to SCCs and provide the mapping.

Next steps: we will prepare (1) a draft DPA addendum and (2) the evidence bundle Naomi described. Julie — please confirm whether the 12-month window and 10 business day SLA meet Novamed's audit needs or if you require a different baseline.

title: Kestrel Health — log retention / proof-of-delete clarifications
preview:
From: Samir Patel <secops@kestrelhealth.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@kestrelhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 22 Nov 2027 09:07:00 -0800
Subject: Initial: Log retention for tenant-scoped debug artifacts

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the demo last week — Kestrel's infra team had one follow-up for procurement/security: for our dedicated/private deployment, we need clarity on which server-side logs and transient artifacts Redwood retains, for how long, and what a customer-facing "proof of deletion" looks like after a data purge request. Specifically: 

- Which classes of logs are tenant-scoped (i.e., include customer identifiers) vs. global/system traces?
- What's the default retention window for tenant-scoped inference traces and KV cache snapshots?
- For SOC 2 / SIG evidence we may be asked for a sample proof-of-delete or a play-by-play that shows deletion completed for a tenant — can you point us to what Redwood can provide (audit IDs, export of deletion job run, preserved manifests)?

Legal asked that any sample evidence be redacted and delivered via a secure portal. Can you confirm whether this is supported and who on your side we should coordinate with?

Appreciate a short reply or a proposed time for a 30-minute workshop with your security contact and our SecOps.

Regards,
Samir Patel
Security Operations
Kestrel Health



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Patel <secops@kestrelhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 22 Nov 2027 11:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial: Log retention for tenant-scoped debug artifacts

Hi Samir — thanks for the clear list. Looping in Priyom (security) and Ben (account lead) so we can get you concrete artifacts and propose next steps. Short answers up front:

- Tenant-scoped logs: inference request metadata, routing headers, and debug traces tied to a tenant id are considered tenant-scoped. Global/system traces (node health, infra metrics) are not emitted with tenant IDs.
- Default retention: hosted API: 30 days for request traces (configurable); Dedicated/Private: defaults to 90 days but can be configured per-contract.
- Proof-of-delete: we can provide a deletion job manifest (job id + timestamps) and an audit record showing the purge completed. For private deployments we can run an on-site evidence collection with redaction.

We have a standard mapping matrix (SIG / CAIQ rows -> artifact types) that Priyom will attach. Ben — can you propose 2 windows for a 30-min workshop with Kestrel's SecOps?

Thanks,
Aisha
Account Executive — Redwood Inference


From: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Patel <secops@kestrelhealth.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 22 Nov 2027 13:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial: Log retention for tenant-scoped debug artifacts

Samir / Aisha — attaching a compact mapping and a short description of artifacts we can produce. High level: 

1) Mapping (attached: kestrel_log_mapping_20271122.xlsx) — maps common SIG/CAIQ entries to Redwood artifacts (audit log, deletion manifest, retention policy config, export of redaction script).

2) Deletion artifacts we can deliver:
   - Deletion job manifest (job_id, start/end timestamps, operator id)
   - Storage tombstone / object lifecycle events where applicable (for S3-backed metadata)
   - Audit events showing the purge command and completion
   - Optional redacted sample of the deleted row set (if you want to validate contents)

3) For Dedicated / Private: we support either a self-service evidence export (customers pull from the evidence portal) or a secured delivery from Redwood. We will redact PII in any samples before delivery.

Notes / caveats:
- Proof-of-delete for ephemeral KV caches: we rotate and zero-out ephemeral caches; we can provide a hashed manifest confirming the zeroing operation, but not a raw dump of ephemeral memory.
- For customers on air-gapped/private installs, evidence collection may require a job run and signed handoff that Kestrel ops signs off on.

I'll attach the mapping now. If you want, we can cover the deletion manifest fields during the workshop and run through a sample that has all redactions applied.

--
Priyom Das
Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference


From: Samir Patel <secops@kestrelhealth.com>
To: Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: Emily Zhou <emily.zhou@kestrelhealth.com>, Kestrel Health Legal <legal@kestrelhealth.com>
Date: Tue, 23 Nov 2027 08:40:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial: Log retention for tenant-scoped debug artifacts

Thanks Priyom — that mapping looks useful. A couple of follow-ups before we schedule: 

- For the deletion manifest, can you confirm the minimum fields we will see? Specifically: job_id, operator, bucket/path affected, object keys (hashed), start/end, and the per-tenant item count.
- For KV-cache zeroing: is the hashed manifest guaranteed stable across runs (i.e., we can compare a pre/post hash)? Or is there any non-determinism we should call out?
- Customer-specific exception: our Legal would like to know whether Kestrel can request a temporary retention extension for troubleshooting (30–90 days) and, if approved, how Redwood documents and enforces that exception for audit purposes. Who signs off on that exception and what artifact proves the exception existed?

If you can reply with the minimum manifest schema and the exception workflow, we'll confirm participants for the workshop (SecOps + Legal + procurement).

Samir



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Patel <secops@kestrelhealth.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.ai>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 23 Nov 2027 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Initial: Log retention for tenant-scoped debug artifacts

Samir — appreciate the specific asks. Next steps I propose:

1) Workshop slots: Wed 11/24 11:00–11:30 PT or Thu 11/25 09:30–10:00 PT. Please pick one and we'll send a calendar invite.
2) Priyom will post the minimum manifest schema in the workshop invite and attach the redacted sample (sample_proof_of_delete.txt).
3) Exception workflow: Redwood documents any approved retention exception as a signed addendum (operator id + approval timestamp + scope + TTL). That addendum becomes part of the audit trail; we also add an "exception" flag in the per-tenant retention config and the audit events reflect the approval. I'll include our standard exception addendum template in the portal.

Delivery: we'll upload artifacts to the secure evidence portal (https://redwood-inference.app/evidence-portal/kstl-12345) after the workshop. Ben

--
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
Mobile: +1 415-555-0192


(attachments referenced in thread: kestrel_log_mapping_20271122.xlsx, sample_proof_of_delete.txt)

title: SOC 2 Type II report access & trust artifacts — next steps
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Jones <emma.jones@greenskyhealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-11-21T10:12:00-08:00
Subject: SOC 2 Type II report access & trust artifacts — next steps

Hi Emma — great to meet you on the call this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps from our side related to compliance artifacts: 

1) What we can share today: attached is a short one-page summary of Redwood's current SOC 2 posture (SOC2_Summary_2027Q3.pdf) and a one‑pager on our attestation cadence.

2) Full report access: we can provide the full SOC 2 Type II report (for the period Nov 2026–May 2027) under a standard NDA / confidentiality path. If you prefer, we can also provide an interim attestation / bridge letter while the full report is routed via the Trust Portal.

3) Trust Portal access: to speed things up, we can provision an account for GreenSky's procurement group so you can download reports and artifact packages. If you can share the names / emails of anyone who should have view access (or confirm procurement@greenskyhealth.com is the right inbox), we’ll kick that off.

4) Vendor security questionnaire (DSQ/DDQ): send us the link or attach the questionnaire and we’ll have our security team respond within 3 business days for standard questionnaires. For custom procurement forms, please flag any required evidence or signed attestations up front.

5) HIPAA / KMS details: if HIPAA controls are a gating item, please let us know the specific fields you need (e.g., BAAs, data residency, key management details) and we’ll pull the relevant docs.

Next steps I propose: 
- You confirm whether you need the full Type II report vs. an interim bridge letter.
- Share the procurement contact(s) for Trust Portal access.
- Paste/attach the vendor questionnaire or share a link.

If you’re good with that, I’ll coordinate with security and Monica to provision portal access and get a bridge letter drafted for signature.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter | Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0142

Attachments:
- SOC2_Summary_2027Q3.pdf (pdf)
- Redwood_TypeII_Roadmap.txt (text)

Thanks Ben — helpful recap. A few clarifying items from procurement/security here:

- A bridge letter/interim attestation will speed our review; please include the effective period and any carve-outs.

Can you also create a shared folder for GreenSky with the following naming convention: GreenskyHealth_Redwood_Artifacts_202711? Target time: end of day 2027-11-22.

2) Expected output golds: we can provide about 20 annotated summaries next week; do you need them in a specific schema?

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>

Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Nov 2027 08:45:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Model fit prompt warehouse & redaction checklist

Hi Sam —

Thanks — quick model + benchmark plan so you have context while you prep samples. High-level recommendations based on the workload you described (clinical summarization, triage assistant):

Recommended model candidates (for evaluation):
- redwood-instruct-7b-q4f: best cost/throughput tradeoff for batch evals; expect higher factuality with careful prompt engineering.
- redwood-alpha-1.1-8k: stronger on longer-context summarization and instruction following; higher cost.
- rw-quant-13b-8k (quantized profile): try for edge-case robustness if latency and budget allow.

Benchmarks / metrics we'll run:
- ROUGE/F1 against your golds (for structured summary metrics)
- Human-rated faithfulness + hallucination index (annotator rubric we can share)
- Regression tests using your edge-case set (we'll flag any >10% degradation vs baseline)

Prompt set guidance (columns in the template):
- prompt_id, original_text, user_intent_label, expected_summary, redaction_needed(boolean), notes_on_edge_case

Security/compliance notes: for any samples with direct identifiers we recommend either:
- uploading to your Private deployment (we can provide instructions), or
- replace identifiers with consistent tokens (e.g., [PATIENT_123]) and provide a mapping offline for internal evals.

I'll drop a short eval workbook to the shared folder with metric definitions and a sample scoring sheet. Once we have the samples we can provision a 48-hour Dedicated endpoint and run the candidate models in parallel.

Links: evaluation workbook (view only) - https://drive.example.com/drive/folders/1a2B_prompt-warehouse-recs

—Marissa

Redwood Inference

title: Async job finalization SLA carveout — proposed service-level language
preview:
From: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@borealis-analytics.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Cc: SRE Team <sre@borealis-analytics.com>, Procurement <procurement@borealis-analytics.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Nov 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Nightly async job SLA — completion window request

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for yesterday's call. As we discussed, Borealis runs a suite of nightly batch inference jobs (approx. 1.2M documents / night) that feed our downstream analytics pipelines. Our compliance and downstream SLAs require us to have these jobs finalized within a 6-hour window after job start; repeated late completions are a showstopper.

We understand Redwood's standard async SLA is oriented around best-effort completion with monitoring and credits for uptime, but we need a documented carveout/exception that: 1) guarantees job finalization for our \"nightly\" workload within 6 hours at P95 under normal utilization, 2) defines retry behavior and idempotency semantics so we can safely re-run parts of the job without duplication, and 3) clarifies monitoring/alerting and the credit remediation formula if the carveout is violated.

Can you send a proposed service-level amendment and a short operational runbook for our SREs (idempotency tokens, safe retry windows, and recommended client backoff behavior)? Procurement wants the clause to be explicit about credits (not just \"commercially reasonable\").

Appreciate any examples of how Redwood has handled similar carveouts for other enterprise customers.

Best,
Tom
Tom Ellis
Head of Data Platform — Borealis Analytics
--
Attachment: nightly-job-metrics.csv (stub, 1.2M rows)


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@borealis-analytics.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 10 Nov 2027 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Nightly async job SLA — completion window request

Hi Tom — thanks for the clear ask and the metrics sample. I'm looping in Laura (Commercial Contracts) and Rachel (Product — SLOs) to draft an actionable carveout.

Quick clarifying q's for the SRE runbook:
- Job start signal: do you always kick these jobs from your side (single scheduled orchestrator) or do you rely on many independent triggers?
- Do you have an existing idempotency token per document or per job-run?

We'll assemble: a proposed amendment (SLA language + credit formula), a short runbook for safe retries and idempotency guidance, and a monitoring/alerting checklist. Expect a first draft by Monday.

Cheers,
Aditya Rao
Sr. Account Executive — Redwood Inference
--
Attachment: none


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 11 Nov 2027 08:30:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Nightly async job SLA — carveout language (internal draft)

Folks — drafting language and commercial remedy. Draft below for review; I'll clean up into a tracked Word doc for procurement.

----- Forwarded message -----
From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 11 Nov 2027 08:28:00 -0800
Subject: Proposed SLA carveout for nightly async job finalization

Proposed clause (summary):
1) Service-level objective: For the scheduled \"nightly-batch\" job (document count ~1.2M), Redwood will target job finalization within six (6) hours from job start at a P95 level, measured over calendar-month rolling windows, provided the customer adheres to the configuration and preconditions in Section 2.

2) Preconditions and operational controls: The following must be true for the target to apply:
   - Customer triggers the job via a single orchestrator endpoint and includes a consistent run_id in the request payload.
   - Customer attaches per-document idempotency_token (UUID) for any re-submitted item; for partial replays, the same idempotency_token must be used.
   - Requests use the documented chunking (<= 10k documents per RPC) and recommended concurrency profile; Redwood will provide a batch adapter config.

3) Retry & idempotency behavior:
   - Redwood will attempt server-side retries for transient infra errors up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) for RPC-level failures.
   - Customer-driven retries are supported; Redwood guarantees idempotent processing provided idempotency_token rules above are followed. Duplicate detection window: 72 hours after initial processing.

4) Monitoring, reporting & remediation:
   - Redwood will publish a per-run completion timestamp to the customer's S3/event sink and emit an alert when in-progress jobs exceed 5 hours.
   - Credit formula: if monthly P95 > 6 hours for the agreed nightly job, Redwood will provide a service credit equal to 5% of that month’s invoice for each additional 6-hour violation bracket, capped at 30% for the month. Exceptions for force majeure/maintenance are excluded when notified per the maintenance window policy.

5) Operational runbook (high level):
   - Detect: alert at 5 hours in-progress; collect trace id and run_id.

Subject: Nightly async job SLA — completion window request

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for yesterday's call. As we discussed, Borealis runs a suite of nightly batch inference jobs (approx. 1.2M documents / night) that feed our downstream analytics pipelines. Our compliance and downstream SLAs require us to have these jobs finalized within a 6-hour window after job start; repeated late completions are a showstopper.

We understand Redwood's standard async SLA is oriented around best-effort completion with monitoring and credits for uptime, but we need a documented carveout/exception that: 1) guarantees job finalization for our \"nightly\" workload within 6 hours at P95 under normal utilization, 2) defines retry behavior and idempotency semantics so we can safely re-run parts of the job without duplication, and 3) clarifies monitoring/alerting and the credit remediation formula if the carveout is violated.

Can you send a proposed service-level amendment and a short operational runbook for our SREs (idempotency tokens, safe retry windows, and recommended client backoff behavior)? Procurement wants the clause to be explicit about credits (not just \"commercially reasonable\").

Appreciate any examples of how Redwood has handled similar carveouts for other enterprise customers.

Best,
Tom

Head of Data Platform — Borealis Analytics

From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>

Subject: Fwd: Nightly async job SLA — carveout language (internal draft)

Folks — drafting language and commercial remedy. Draft below for review; I'll clean up into a tracked Word doc for procurement.

----- Forwarded message -----
From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 11 Nov 2027 08:28:00 -0800
Subject: Proposed SLA carveout for nightly async job finalization

Proposed clause (summary):
1) Service-level objective: For the scheduled \"nightly-batch\" job (document count ~1.2M), Redwood will target job finalization within six (6) hours from job start at a P95 level, measured over calendar-month rolling windows, provided the customer adheres to the configuration and preconditions in Section 2.

2) Preconditions and operational controls: The following must be true for the target to apply:
   - Customer triggers the job via a single orchestrator endpoint and includes a consistent run_id in the request payload.
   - Customer attaches per-document idempotency_token (UUID) for any re-submitted item; for partial replays, the same idempotency_token must be used.
   - Requests use the documented chunking (<= 10k documents per RPC) and recommended concurrency profile; Redwood will provide a batch adapter config.

3) Retry & idempotency behavior:
   - Redwood will attempt server-side retries for transient infra errors up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) for RPC-level failures.
   - Customer-driven retries are supported; Redwood guarantees idempotent processing provided idempotency_token rules above are followed. Duplicate detection window: 72 hours after initial processing.

4) Monitoring, reporting & remediation:
   - Redwood will publish a per-run completion timestamp to the customer's S3/event sink and emit an alert when in-progress jobs exceed 5 hours.
   - Credit formula: if monthly P95 > 6 hours for the agreed nightly job, Redwood will provide a service credit equal to 5% of that month’s invoice for each additional 6-hour violation bracket, capped at 30% for the month. Exceptions for force majeure/maintenance are excluded when notified per the maintenance window policy.

5) Operational runbook (high level):
   - Detect: alert at 5 hours in-progress; collect trace id and run_id.
   - Contain: if the issue is due to backpressure, automatically escalate to Redwood Dedicated pool autoscale policy; if persistent, trigger fallback to lower-cost model variant (per agreed fallbacks).
   - Recover: re-run job segments using idempotency_token; reconcile downstream offsets.

Please flag any legal/commercial issues; Ben, any redlines on credits? Rachel — please confirm the feasibility of the 6-hour P95 target under a 1.2M document night on N4 GPU pool profile.

Laura
VP, Commercial Contracts

title: MAP close checklist — performance runbook & tradeoff log
preview:
From: Julia Park <julia@healthlytics.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Darren Miles <darren@acmehealth.org>
Date: Wed, 10 Nov 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: MAP: perf targets & timeline questions

Ben —

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday. We (HealthLytics) are aligned on the MAP items but need to lock the performance gating before legal and procurement will sign. Two quick asks so we can finalize the mutual action plan this week:

1) Can you send a concise runbook that shows the exact perf metrics you will collect during evaluation (latency P50/P95, TTFT, throughput @X concurrency, GPU util, and a simple quality pass rate for our QA set)?
2) We need a proposed close timeline that ties the MAP milestones to a final procurement sign-off (targeting Nov 30).

If you can include notes on model variants (e.g., tradeoffs between smaller quantized models vs larger FP16 variants) that would be ideal — our CTO wants to understand the pragmatic tradeoffs for a clinical summarization pipeline.

Appreciate it — happy to hop on a quick call if helpful.

— Julia
Julia Park
Head of Integrations, HealthLytics
julia@healthlytics.io

(Attachment: HL-qa-set-v2.json)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Julia Park <julia@healthlytics.io>
Cc: Darren Miles <darren@acmehealth.org>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Nov 2027 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP: perf targets & timeline questions

Julia —

Thanks — great questions. I put together an operational runbook and a proposed timeline below. High level summary up front: we recommend a two-week eval window with parallel variant runs (quantized small model + FP16 medium model) to hit your Nov 30 procurement target. We will provide the raw perf CSV and a summary scorecard at the end of the window.

Runbook (what we will collect):
- Latency: P50, P95 measured at request-level (end-to-end) including network (ms)
- TTFT: time-to-first-token from request receipt (ms)
- Throughput: tokens/sec and requests/sec under sustained load (varied qps)
- Concurrency experiments: runs at 1, 8, 32, 64 concurrent streams; measure tail latency and degradation
- Resource metrics: GPU utilization, memory, and host CPU
- Quality: pass rate on your QA set + semantic similarity (embedding cosine) vs. reference (we'll report pass/fail and a small confusion table)

Variant matrix (examples we will test):
- redwood-base-13b (FP16) — best quality / higher cost
- redwood-13b-q4 (quantized int4) — lower cost / faster, small quality delta on summarization
- community-7b-opt (FP16) — low cost, variable quality on clinical text

Deliverables:
- Raw perf export (CSV) and a condensed performance scorecard (one-page)
- MAP timeline doc with gating criteria for procurement (pass rate >= 90%, P95 < 300ms at 8 concurrent streams)

Proposed timeline (aligned to MAP milestones):
- Nov 12–13: baseline ingestion & QA set dry run (no load)
- Nov 14–18: parallel variant perf runs (concurrency sweep)
- Nov 19: Redwood shares perf CSV + scorecard, embedded link to Drive
- Nov 20–24: joint review + optional follow-up runs if a variant needs tuning
- Nov 25: final MAP signoff document sent to HealthLytics legal/procurement
- Nov 30: procurement execution (target)

I've looped in Rishi (serving) and Marissa (solutions) to confirm the gating thresholds and to run the initial experiments on our end. I'm attaching the proposed MAP timeline PDF for your tracker.

If these milestones look good, we'll schedule the first run on Nov 12.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
(Attachment: map_timeline_20271114.pdf)

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Nov 2027 08:23:00 -0800
Subject: RE: MAP: perf targets & timeline questions

Ben —

Quick note from infra/serving side about the experiment plan and gating thresholds. We ran an internal dry-run on Nov 11 with a copy of HealthLytics' QA set (sanitized) and here are the pragmatic expectations: 

Experiment config (Nov 12–18):
- Load pattern: steady QPS ramp (0 -> target) then sustained 5 min windows per concurrency bucket
- Concurrency buckets: 1, 8, 32, 64
- Payload: avg request 220 tokens, response target ~120 tokens

Preliminary internal results (single-run summary):
- redwood-base-13b (FP16): P50=85ms, P95=220ms, TTFT=45ms, throughput @8=140 req/s, GPU util=72%
- redwood-13b-q4: P50=60ms, P95=280ms, TTFT=35ms, throughput @8=210 req/s, GPU util=61%
- community-7b-opt: P50=50ms, P95=420ms, TTFT=30ms, throughput @8=260 req/s, GPU util=48%

Quality notes (on HealthLytics QA v2 subset; pass rate = exact/acceptable summary):
- redwood-base-13b (FP16): pass rate 93% (best semantics, minimal hallucination on clinical abbreviations)
- redwood-13b-q4: pass rate 89% (slight brevity/condensing differences on multi-paragraph notes)
- community-7b-opt: pass rate 76% (issues with formatting and clinical term fidelity)

Recommendation (short):
- Gate on pass rate >= 90% and P95 <= 300ms at 8 concurrent streams for production. That implies redwood-base-13b FP16 meets quality but with higher cost; redwood-13b-q4 may be acceptable if HealthLytics accepts occasional brevity tuning and we run a short follow-up tuning pass.

I'm uploading the raw CSV from the dry run and can run the full concurrency sweep on Nov 14 per Ben's timeline.

— Rishi
Rishi Malhotra
Principal, Serving Infrastructure — Redwood Inference
rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com
(Attachment: redwood_perf_run_20271112.csv)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Julia Park <julia@healthlytics.io>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Nov 2027 11:08:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP: perf targets & timeline questions

Julia —

Sharing Rishi's preliminary numbers above and the plan to run the full concurrency sweep today (Nov 14). Quick callout on tradeoffs: the quantized 13b-q4 buys ~35% throughput improvement with small quality delta (~4 points on pass rate) — we recommend a short tuning step (prompt/temperature and few-shot template) to recover most of that gap. 

Proposed acceptance criteria for MAP (to feed procurement):
- Quality: pass rate >= 90% on your QA set (primary gate)
- Latency: P95 <= 300ms at 8 concurrent streams
- TTFT: <= 60ms (for interactive UX)
- Stability: no more than 2% error responses under sustained load

I've attached the one-page MAP timeline and will post the full perf CSV to the Drive link in a few hours. If HealthLytics is ok with the acceptance criteria and proposed timeline we will mark the MAP as 'in evaluation' today and proceed with the runs.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
(Attachment: map_timeline_20271114.pdf)

From: Julia Park <julia@healthlytics.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Darren Miles <darren@acmehealth.org>
Date: Mon, 14 Nov 2027 16:23:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP: perf targets & timeline questions

Ben / Rishi —

This looks good and actionable. The acceptance criteria align with our internal bar; we especially appreciate the explicit TTFT target. Please proceed with the concurrency sweep and share the Drive link when the CSV is live. We'll plan procurement review for Nov 25 contingent on the scorecard.

One small ask — can you add a short note in the MAP doc that outlines the fallback policy (if quantized variant underperforms in production, Redwood will automatically route to FP16 variant until follow-up tuning is complete)? That helps our procurement folks with risk assessment.

Thanks,
Julia
Julia Park
Head of Integrations, HealthLytics
julia@healthlytics.io

- Latency: P95 <= 300ms at 8 concurrent streams
- TTFT: <= 60ms (for interactive UX)
- Stability: no more than 2% error responses under sustained load

I've attached the one-page MAP timeline and will post the full perf CSV to the Drive link in a few hours. If HealthLytics is ok with the acceptance criteria and proposed timeline we will mark the MAP as 'in evaluation' today and proceed with the runs.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
(Attachment: map_timeline_20271114.pdf)

Cc: Darren Miles <darren@acmehealth.org>
Date: Mon, 14 Nov 2027 16:23:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP: perf targets & timeline questions

Ben / Rishi —

This looks good and actionable. The acceptance criteria align with our internal bar; we especially appreciate the explicit TTFT target. Please proceed with the concurrency sweep and share the Drive link when the CSV is live. We'll plan procurement review for Nov 25 contingent on the scorecard.

One small ask — can you add a short note in the MAP doc that outlines the fallback policy (if quantized variant underperforms in production, Redwood will automatically route to FP16 variant until follow-up tuning is complete)? That helps our procurement folks with risk assessment.

Thanks,
Julia

julia@healthlytics.io

Subject: FY28 target-setting sprint — timeline & risk scenarios

Team —

As discussed in last week's leadership sync, it's time to run a short, focused planning sprint to: 1) land FY28 targets by org, 2) run two downside/upside scenarios for revenue and capacity, and 3) produce a QBR-ready one-pager for exec review.

Proposed cadence (week-by-week):
- Week 1 (Nov 8–12): Data pull + baseline targets from each function (revenue ops, product, infra).
- Week 2 (Nov 15–19): Scenario modeling (base / conservative / upside) + cost levers.
- Week 3 (Nov 22–26): Consolidation, QBR slide deck draft, leadership review.

Ask: please confirm owners for the baseline deliverables by EOD Monday Nov 8. I've attached a scenario workbook that contains templates for revenue curves, headcount sensitivity, and cost-per-token levers.

title: Koto Financial — encryption & data-handling follow-up + workshop proposal
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Miyu Tanaka <miyu.tanaka@koto.io>, Koto Security <security@koto.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 9 Nov 2027 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Koto Financial — encryption & data-handling follow-up + workshop proposal

Hi Miyu / Koto security team,

Thanks again for the call earlier — helpful to understand the regulatory and operational constraints you're balancing. I wanted to capture the key asks we heard and propose concrete next steps so we have alignment ahead of a technical workshop.

Recap of the ask / risk areas you raised:
- PII handling: ability to delete customer PII on-demand and limit retention to configurable windows per customer.
- Data residency: production datasets must remain in us-east-1 for primary operations; APAC environment considered later (phase 2).
- Logging & audit: 90-day application logs, 7-year audit evidence availability for legal requests; streaming audit logs to your SIEM is required.
- Encryption: TLS 1.2+ in transit; AES-256 at rest. Production must support customer-managed keys (BYOK) with HSM-backed key custody for keys of record.
- Access control: SAML SSO + SCIM provisioning for service accounts and RBAC controls for admin operations.

Proposed next steps:
1) Technical workshop (60–90m) with your infra/security and our infra/crypto engineers — suggested slots below.
2) Koto to share the filled security questionnaire (or we can accept answers as a pre-brief).
3) Redwood to provide: SOC2 snapshot, network diagram, and a concise BYOK/HSM integration checklist before the workshop.
4) Pre-work: confirm preferred KMS vendor (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or on-prem HSM) and whether key material import is required.

Suggested workshop times (PT): Thu 11/11 10:00–11:30, Fri 11/12 09:00–10:30 — please confirm a slot and attendee list.

Attaching: redwood_security_onepager.pdf with a one-page summary of our controls and a short encryption integration primer.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0182

---
redwood_security_onepager.pdf (attachment stub)

From: Miyu Tanaka <miyu.tanaka@koto.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Koto Security <security@koto.io>, Harper Lim <harper.lim@koto.io>
Date: Tue, 9 Nov 2027 13:42:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Koto Financial — encryption & data-handling follow-up + workshop proposal

Hi Ava,

Appreciate the clear recap. A few clarifications and answers from our side to help scope the workshop:

Quick answers:
- BYOK: required for production environments that process PII. We expect HSM-backed key custody (CloudHSM or equivalent) and an auditable separation of duties for key ops.
- KMS preference: Primary is AWS KMS with CloudHSM-backed keys; we will consider GCP KMS only for a dedicated APAC tenant later.
- In-transit: TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred.
- At-rest: AES-256 with root keys stored in HSM.
- PII retention: policy goal is to retain PII for no longer than 180 days by default; certain regulated flows (e.g., KYC) may require longer retention — we'll mark that in the questionnaire.
- Audit logs: we want export of audit events to our SIEM (S3 or direct ingestion) and 7-year retention for legal/compliance requests (redwood can have shorter token logs if you provide an immutable audit trail to us).

Attachments: I've attached our security requirements and the completed questionnaire (koto_security_requirements.pdf, koto_sso_scim_and_questionnaire.xlsx). The questionnaire includes a handful of legal/contract asks (encryption attestation language, where to place key-rotation SLAs) — legal will follow up if needed.

Workshop: Thu 11/11 10:00–11:30 PT works for our infra/security team. Proposed attendees: myself (security lead), Harper Lim (infra), Rafael (app sec) — I'll send a calendar invite shortly.

A few items we would like Redwood to bring to the workshop:
- A short runbook describing BYOK onboarding (key import vs. KMS grants).
- Network egress/connectivity plan and a minimal allowlist for test vs prod.
- Examples for rotating keys and emergency key destruction procedures.

Thanks — looking forward.

Best,
Miyu
Miyu Tanaka
Head of Security, Koto Financial
miyu.tanaka@koto.io

---
koto_security_requirements.pdf (attachment stub)

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Miyu Tanaka <miyu.tanaka@koto.io>, Koto Security <security@koto.io>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 9 Nov 2027 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Koto Financial — encryption & data-handling follow-up + workshop proposal

Hi Miyu / Koto security team,

Thanks again for the call earlier — helpful to understand the regulatory and operational constraints you're balancing. I wanted to capture the key asks we heard and propose concrete next steps so we have alignment ahead of a technical workshop.

Recap of the ask / risk areas you raised:
- PII handling: ability to delete customer PII on-demand and limit retention to configurable windows per customer.
- Data residency: production datasets must remain in us-east-1 for primary operations; APAC environment considered later (phase 2).
- Logging & audit: 90-day application logs, 7-year audit evidence availability for legal requests; streaming audit logs to your SIEM is required.
- Encryption: TLS 1.2+ in transit; AES-256 at rest. Production must support customer-managed keys (BYOK) with HSM-backed key custody for keys of record.
- Access control: SAML SSO + SCIM provisioning for service accounts and RBAC controls for admin operations.

Proposed next steps:
1) Technical workshop (60–90m) with your infra/security and our infra/crypto engineers — suggested slots below.
2) Koto to share the filled security questionnaire (or we can accept answers as a pre-brief).
3) Redwood to provide: SOC2 snapshot, network diagram, and a concise BYOK/HSM integration checklist before the workshop.
4) Pre-work: confirm preferred KMS vendor (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or on-prem HSM) and whether key material import is required.

Suggested workshop times (PT): Thu 11/11 10:00–11:30, Fri 11/12 09:00–10:30 — please confirm a slot and attendee list.

Attaching: redwood_security_onepager.pdf with a one-page summary of our controls and a short encryption integration primer.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0182

From: Miyu Tanaka <miyu.tanaka@koto.io>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Koto Security <security@koto.io>, Harper Lim <harper.lim@koto.io>
Date: Tue, 9 Nov 2027 13:42:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Koto Financial — encryption & data-handling follow-up + workshop proposal

Hi Ava,

Appreciate the clear recap. A few clarifications and answers from our side to help scope the workshop:

Quick answers:
- BYOK: required for production environments that process PII. We expect HSM-backed key custody (CloudHSM or equivalent) and an auditable separation of duties for key ops.
- KMS preference: Primary is AWS KMS with CloudHSM-backed keys; we will consider GCP KMS only for a dedicated APAC tenant later.
- In-transit: TLS 1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 preferred.
- At-rest: AES-256 with root keys stored in HSM.
- PII retention: policy goal is to retain PII for no longer than 180 days by default; certain regulated flows (e.g., KYC) may require longer retention — we'll mark that in the questionnaire.
- Audit logs: we want export of audit events to our SIEM (S3 or direct ingestion) and 7-year retention for legal/compliance requests (redwood can have shorter token logs if you provide an immutable audit trail to us).

Attachments: I've attached our security requirements and the completed questionnaire (koto_security_requirements.pdf, koto_sso_scim_and_questionnaire.xlsx). The questionnaire includes a handful of legal/contract asks (encryption attestation language, where to place key-rotation SLAs) — legal will follow up if needed.

Workshop: Thu 11/11 10:00–11:30 PT works for our infra/security team. Proposed attendees: myself (security lead), Harper Lim (infra), Rafael (app sec) — I'll send a calendar invite shortly.

A few items we would like Redwood to bring to the workshop:
- A short runbook describing BYOK onboarding (key import vs. KMS grants).
- Network egress/connectivity plan and a minimal allowlist for test vs prod.
- Examples for rotating keys and emergency key destruction procedures.

Thanks — looking forward.

Best,
Miyu
Miyu Tanaka
Head of Security, Koto Financial
miyu.tanaka@koto.io

title: MSA / Order Form — dispute-paths playbook for governing law & injunctive relief (CorpIgnite)
preview:
From: Dana Morris <dana.morris@corpignite.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evan Cole <evan.cole@corpignite.com>, legal@corpignite.com
Date: Mon, 8 Nov 2027 10:14:00 -0800
Subject: MSA redline — governing law & injunctive relief (CorpIgnite)

Ben — thanks for the draft MSA and order form. Our GC has flagged two sticking points in the dispute section that will block procurement sign-off unless we can find middle ground: governing law and the carve for equitable/injunctive relief.

Key items on our side:
- We prefer Delaware law or, failing that, New York (consistency with other vendor contracts).
- For injunctive relief, CorpIgnite needs the ability to seek emergency relief in Delaware courts (IP/data preservation are central to our risk profile).
- We are OK with arbitration for monetary claims, but our legal team wants the court carve for injunctive relief preserved as written.

I've attached our redline (CorpIgnite_MSA_redline_v3.docx) with comments. Can Redwood indicate appetite for: (a) switching governing law to DE/NY, or (b) leaving CA but conceding courts for emergency injunctive relief in Delaware?

We are on a tight timeline — procurement hopes to finish by end of next week. Happy to hop on a short call to resolve quickly.

Thanks,
Dana Morris
Head of Procurement, CorpIgnite
attachments: [CorpIgnite_MSA_redline_v3.docx]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dana Morris <dana.morris@corpignite.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 8 Nov 2027 11:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MSA redline — governing law & injunctive relief (CorpIgnite)

Dana — really appreciate the clear redline and timeline. Looping in Kimberly and Laura from Legal to propose an operational playbook so procurement can close on schedule.

Proposed next steps from Sales:
- Quick triage call (30m) between our teams tomorrow to walk the redline and confirm the exact carve language you need.
- If compromise is straightforward, legal to produce a compromise redline same day.
- If not, escalate to VP Sales / GC for final decision (we prefer to avoid).

We are generally amenable to court carveouts for injunctive relief for IP/data preservation but prefer to keep governing law in our standard form where possible to reduce precedent risk. That said, we can explore a limited exception tied to: (i) narrow scope (injunctive relief only), (ii) forum specified, and (iii) reciprocal emergency relief language.

Available tomorrow 10:00–12:00 PT or 14:00–16:00 PT — which works for you?

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
attachments: []

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dana Morris <dana.morris@corpignite.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 9 Nov 2027 09:18:00 -08:00
Subject: RE: MSA redline — governing law & injunctive relief (CorpIgnite)

Dana / Ben — thanks for the note and redline. I reviewed CorpIgnite_MSA_redline_v3 and sketched an alternative compromise below intended to be operationally predictable and legally narrow. We can convert to tracked changes after we confirm the approach.

Proposed compromise (summary):
- Governing law: State of Washington (Washington law governs the MSA).
  - Rationale: Washington law provides a neutral balance for both parties and aligns with our corporate counsel’s preference to avoid forum-shopping precedents.
- Dispute resolution (monetary claims): Binding arbitration (JAMS) with seat in King County, WA, administered under the AAA/JAMS rules you prefer.
- Injunctive / equitable relief carve: Either party may seek emergency injunctive or other provisional relief in the state or federal courts located in King County, Washington (Seattle) for matters relating to intellectual property, data preservation, or confidentiality breaches.
  - The carve is strictly limited to emergency provisional measures; all other claims remain subject to arbitration.
- Mutuality: Carve applies reciprocally.
- Service of process / waiver language: minimal service provisions to ensure enforceability of court orders.

If CorpIgnite cannot accept WA as the governing law, we can also propose: governing law = California, with injunctive relief carve permitted in either King County, WA or Delaware (mutually selectable at the time of filing) — but that adds complexity and unpredictability.

Please advise whether CorpIgnite’s counsel would accept Washington as a neutral seat. If not, identify a single fallback (DE or NY) and we’ll draft precise redline language with the narrow injunctive carve described above.

-Kim
Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
attachments: []

From: Dana Morris <dana.morris@corpignite.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evan Cole <evan.cole@corpignite.com>, legal@corpignite.com
Date: Wed, 10 Nov 2027 14:42:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MSA redline — governing law & injunctive relief (CorpIgnite)

Kim / Ben — thanks for the quick review and thoughtful compromise. CorpIgnite’s GC reviewed your Washington proposal. Short response:

- Governing law: Our preference remains Delaware; New York acceptable as second choice. Washington is a non-starter for internal approval because our enterprise legal playbook standardizes on DE/NY for vendor agreements.
- Injunctive carve: We can accept a narrow, reciprocal carve for emergency injunctive relief in courts — provided the carve explicitly permits CorpIgnite to seek relief in Delaware (if we choose) and that Redwood agrees to reciprocal relief in Redwood’s chosen jurisdiction for comparable emergencies.

Suggested near-term compromise:
1) Governing law: Delaware (but limited to substantive contract law only).
2) Arbitration: Monetary claims go to arbitration (JAMS or AAA) with the seat selected by mutual agreement; if no mutual agreement, seat = New York.
3) Injunctive relief carve: Either party may seek emergency injunctive relief in a court of competent jurisdiction; each party may file in its own forum for emergency relief for IP/data preservation. The carve explicitly preserves the other party’s right to move to dismiss or transfer after relief is granted.

Timing: we can accept this as a compromise if Redwood produces tracked changes implementing the language above by EOD tomorrow. Procurement will then route for final sign-off.

If Redwood cannot accept Delaware as governing law, we’ll need to escalate.

Best,
Dana
Dana Morris
Head of Procurement, CorpIgnite
attachments: [CorpIgnite_MSA_redline_v3.docx]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dana Morris <dana.morris@corpignite.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Nov 2027 15:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MSA redline — governing law & injunctive relief (CorpIgnite)

Dana — thanks. Appreciate the flexibility and clear escalation trigger. Kimberly and Laura — can you produce a tracked-redline that: (a) sets governing law to Delaware (substantive law only), (b) keeps arbitration for monetary claims and pins the default seat to New York if mutual seat not agreed, and (c) carves emergency injunctive relief for either party to their home forum (explicitly reciprocal and narrowly targeted to IP/data/confidentiality preservation)?

Proposed timeline: legal redline by EOD tomorrow, call only if there are open material issues after that. If legal cannot accommodate Delaware as governing law, advise and I will escalate to VP Sales and our GC.

Action items:
- Kimberly / Laura: draft tracked changes to implement Dana’s near‑term compromise (Delaware substantive law + reciprocal injunctive carve) — due 2027-11-12 EOD.
- Ben: schedule follow-up review with CorpIgnite Procurement for 2027-11-13 10:00 PT if redline is delivered.

Thanks all — we’re close.

Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Mobile: (415) 555-0192

title: Dedicated capacity amendment — margin buffer and autoscale safeguards
preview:
From: Emily Price <emily.price@heliotech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@heliotech.com>, procurement@heliotech.com
Date: 2027-11-06T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Dedicated capacity amendment — margin buffer and autoscale safeguards

Ben —

Thanks again for the call this morning. We're fast-approaching a staged product launch and need a short-term operational plan to avoid throttling during peak week. Two asks from our side: 1) a temporary margin buffer (headroom) that guarantees an uplift of ~30% sustained throughput for a 6-week window starting 2027-12-01; and 2) explicit autoscale safeguards so we can avoid noisy preemption impacting live inference (SLO is 150ms p95).

Specifically:
- Can Redwood commit to a transient slab model (reserved-but-ephemeral) for that period and provide clear eviction behavior and priority tiers?
- We need billing clarity — how transient uplift will be invoiced (credits vs prorated usage).
- We also need an incident runbook and a named incident contact for the launch window.

I've attached our internal capacity forecast for context (capacity_matrix_v3.xlsx). If you can send a short amendment draft that outlines the uplift model + billing mechanics we can get procurement moving.

Can you confirm by EOD Monday (11/08)? We have a contract review scheduled the following week.

Appreciate it —
Emily Price
Head of Cloud Ops, HelioTech
em: emily.price@heliotech.com
ph: +1 415-555-2198

Attachment: capacity_matrix_v3.xlsx (Excel, 72KB)


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Emily Price <emily.price@heliotech.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-11-06T13:44:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity amendment — margin buffer and autoscale safeguards

Emily —

Thanks for the clear ask and the forecast. We can support a transient uplift model without converting to a permanent reserve. High-level proposal (quick summary):

1) Transient margin buffer (ephemeral slab):
   - Uplift: +30% baseline throughput expressed as a temporary slab allocation for 2027-12-01 to 2028-01-12 (6 weeks).
   - Implementation: dedicated ephemeral pool (preemptible-capable instances) attached to your Dedicated capacity namespace. Pool will be tagged with a higher eviction priority to avoid affecting your core slab.

2) Autoscale & safeguard policy:
   - Canary ramping: new traffic shifts will be canaried to the transient pool with a 72-hour progressive ramp (25% -> 50% -> 75% -> full), with automated rollback triggers at 5% error delta or p95 latency degradation > 20%.
   - Burst credits: we propose a credit-bucket model to smooth short spikes; credits are consumed first before billing uplifts.
   - Min-instance floor: a hard floor (min_instances) for your core slab so that preemptions do not drop below X GPUs (we'll set X based on your baseline to keep p95 target).

3) Billing & commercial mechanics:
   - Option A (preferred for transparency): prorated usage for the ephemeral slab at discounted burst rate, plus a one-time setup credit to cover rapid ramping.
   - Option B: predefined ephemeral hours allocation (credit bucket) applied to the period; excess beyond credits billed at on-demand uplift.

4) Runbook & contacts:
   - We'll deliver a launch runbook with explicit escalation paths. During the 6-week window Redwood will provide an on-call escalation for incidents regarding eviction or latency regressions.

I'll circulate a one-page amendment draft and a more detailed ops runbook by EOD Monday. Marcus and Laura are looped here for the technical/ops sections.

Next step: can we do a 30-minute sync on Wednesday 11/10 to walk the amendment and the runbook? I propose 10:00 PT.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 510-555-0123

Attachments: proposed_amendment_draft.pdf (PDF, 48KB)


From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Emily Price <emily.price@heliotech.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-11-07T08:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity amendment — margin buffer and autoscale safeguards

Emily / Ben —

A few technical clarifications so expectations align:

- Eviction semantics: the ephemeral pool will be composed of preemptible instance types. Eviction is controlled at GPU allocation level; when capacity pressure occurs we will first reclaim ephemeral pool GPUs (L2 eviction) before touching core slab. We'll attach a priority tag so orchestration respects your reserved floor.

- Warmup & cold-start: KV-cache warmup for long-context models can cause a transient latency hit for the first few minutes after a scale event. We recommend a staggered canary (as Ben suggested) and a lightweight prefix-warmup probe (synthetic requests that populate caches) during each ramp stage.

- Cooling window and autoscale hysteresis: set scale-down cooldown to 10m for the ephemeral pool to avoid thrash; scale-up should be immediate but gated by request queue-latency thresholds.

- Headroom calculation: we'll compute headroom as 1.3 * 95th-percentile steady token throughput over the last 7 days, plus a 10% safety margin for burst jitter. This produces a conservative target you can validate with your forecast.

- Fallback routing: in the event the ephemeral pool cannot satisfy requests we will route degraded traffic to a smaller, quantized model variant with a clear degradation profile (lower token throughput but preserved correctness) — included in the amendment as a graceful degradation option.

I'll include these details in the ops runbook and a short ops checklist (pre-launch probes, SLOs, rollback).

—Marcus
Marcus Lin
Principal Systems Engineer, Redwood Inference
marcus@redwood.com


From: Emily Price <emily.price@heliotech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@heliotech.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, procurement@heliotech.com
Date: 2027-11-08T10:21:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity amendment — margin buffer and autoscale safeguards

Ben / Marcus —

This looks reasonable. A couple items procurement needs before we sign:
1) Exact billing example for the two options (sample invoice lines showing credits applied).
2) A rollback clause that allows HelioTech to revert the ephemeral slab early within the 6-week window without penalty if it negatively affects latency or costs exceed thresholds; we suggest a 48-hour notice window for early termination.
3) The ops runbook (including the named on-call escalation and the synthetic warmup probe steps).

We prefer Option A if the sample invoice lines show predictable spend. Also, please include a clause that guarantees the canary rollback will be executed automatically on the defined triggers (error delta, p95 latency).

Procurement has a DocuSign template ready — if you can attach the amendment draft we will route it internally. If you can get the sample invoice and runbook before Wednesday call, procurement can likely sign on the call.

Thanks —
Emily

Attachment: none

Date: 2027-11-09T16:42:00-07:00

Emily —

Great — we've updated the amendment draft to include:
- a sample invoice with Option A and Option B (line items for ephemeral hours, credit consumption, and overage),
- an explicit early-termination clause allowing HelioTech to exit the ephemeral slab with 48 hours' notice and pro-rated billing through termination date,
- automatic canary rollback triggers (5% error delta, p95 > +20% baseline) and an IR contact list.

If that's all good I'll route the final DocuSign envelope and loop the legal teams for a quick sign-off.

Thanks everyone —
Ben
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
